Strong west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the afternoon and evening of the 14th in New Jersey. Peak wind gusts averaged close to 50 mph and tore down weak tree limbs and wires.  In Cumberland County, in Vineland, the strong wind tore off a ten foot by forty foot section of an old facade from a vacant downtown business building on East Landis Avenue. The building has been unoccupied since at least 2005. Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 52 mph in Pert Amboy (Middlesex County) and Brick (Ocean County), 50 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and Barnegat (Ocean County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County) and Hackettstown (Warren County), 47 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 46 mph in Lumberton (Burlington County), Morristown (Morris County) and Millville (Cumberland County). The strong winds were fueled by the pressure difference between the cold front's low pressure system that moved through New England and a high pressure system building east from the western Great Lakes. The strong winds slowly decreased overnight as the low pressure system moved farther away from New Jersey.

Freezing rain fell across central and southern New Jersey overnight on February 1st. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch with the highest accretions in Monmouth County. The freezing rain started in the evening on February 1st. As warmer air moved in overnight, the freezing rain changed to plain rain around 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 2nd. Untreated roadways were hazardous. About 36,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state, but most of these outages occurred in west central and northern New Jersey. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and New York City was disrupted on the 2nd because of icy conditions. Many schools had delayed openings. All New Jersey Department of Motor Vehicle offices were closed at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd because of power outages. Some recycling and trash collections were delayed. This latest winter storm further taxed snow removal budgets, snow days on school calendars and exacerbated the shortage of salt. The onshore flow coupled with the higher astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused pockets of minor tidal flooding along the southeast coast of New Jersey during the early morning high tide cycle on the 2nd. The high tide reached 6.00 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) which is the threshold for minor tidal flooding. The high tide reached 6.73 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||The freezing rain was caused by a combination of a low pressure system that was moving east up the Ohio Valley and a high pressure system that cut across southern Canada. The former provided the rain while the latter helped leave enough cold air in place for freezing to occur overnight on the 1st. Enough warm air moved in at the surface close to sunrise on the 2nd to change the freezing rain to rain.
A strong cold frontal passage during the afternoon on the 25th triggered a squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through central and southern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore off part of a roof from an apartment building on Prospect Street. Families in nine units were evacuated, six families were displaced until repairs were made. At the nearby Kimball Medical Center, downed trees damaged a few vehicles in the parking lot.

A fast moving low pressure system dropped heavy snow over parts of northwest New Jersey and plowable snows as far south as the Raritan Basin in New Jersey. Snow began around Midnight EST in northwest New Jersey and fell at its heaviest between Midnight EST and 6 a.m. EST on the 21st. The snow ended around the middle of the morning on the 21st. The Raritan Basin was along the southern edge of the precipitation shield and had mixed precipitation (some sleet and rain) during the same time period. Snowfall averaged 3 to 7 inches in Northwest New Jersey and 1 to 3 inches in the Raritan Basin. For the second time within a week heavy rain fell across New Jersey, especially the northern half. An additional 2 to 5 inches of precipitation fell and caused continued flooding in the Passaic Basin and renewed flooding on the Delaware and Raritan Rivers. Snowmelt in the upper reaches of the Delaware Basin contributed to the flooding. Major flooding occurred within the Passaic Basin, mostly minor flooding occurred on the Delaware River and in the Raritan Basin. Governor Chris Christie declared a state of emergency before the start of the second round of heavy rain on March 9th.  Throughout the state, 683 dwellings were affected by both flooding events and 207 suffered at least major damage. The second round of rain started during the early morning of the 10th, fell heavy at times during the daylight hours and evening on the 10th and ended between 4 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST on the morning on the 11th. Eastern Daylight Time started on the morning of the 13th. Some of the rivers began flooding during Eastern Standard Time and receded after Eastern Daylight Time began.||South of Trenton on the Delaware River, the combination of the up the Delaware Bay and River wind flow and freshwater runoff produced minor tidal flooding along the river and tidal sections of its tributaries during the late afternoon high tide cycle on the 10th and the early morning high tide cycle on the 11th. The highest tide occurred on the 11th and reached 8.93 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Southern Plains on the morning of the 8th northeast into Iowa on the morning of the 9th, Michigan on the morning of the 10th and into the eastern Great Lakes by the morning of the 11th. This system was able to tap moisture from both the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean and lift it over its frontal boundaries. The deep southeast flow was able to enhance the precipitation further over the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey. The rain ended once the low's occluded front moved through New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along the Delaware River washed out eighty feet of River Road in Pohatcong Township. In Harmony Township, river flooding occurred along Hutchinson River Road. A few homes were surrounded and a few residents were evacuated from the flood waters. The Delaware River at Tocks Island was above its 21 foot flood stage from 416 p.m. EST on the 11th through 956 a.m. EST on the 12th. It crested at 22.88 feet at 1245 a.m. EST on the 11th. Farther downstream, the Delaware River at Phillipsburg had moderate flooding. The river was above its 22 foot flood stage from 914 a.m. EST on the 11th through 538 p.m. EST on the 12th. It crested at 26.11 feet at 1245 a.m. EST on the 12th. Farther downstream, the Delaware River at Riegelsville was above its 22 foot flood stage from 1041 a.m. EST on the 11th through 625 p.m. EST on the 12th. It crested at 25.20 feet at 145 a.m. EST on the 12th.||Event precipitation totals included 3.05 inches in Franklin, 2.88 inches in White, 2.76 inches in Oxford, 2.73 inches in Greenwich, 2.69 inches in Hackettstown, 2.51 inches in Stewartsville, 2.38 inches in Mansfield, 2.30 inches in Washington, 2.13 inches in Allamuchy, 2.01 inches in Blairstown, 1.87 inches in Hope and 1.85 inches in Liberty.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system tracked northeast from the Lower Mississippi River Valley into the Ohio Valley Tuesday, February 1st into Tuesday Night, with a secondary low pressure developing over the Delmarva region and tracking southeast of Long Island on Wednesday February 2nd.  With a polar high locked in place over Ontario and Quebec, this slow moving storm system brought a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region.  A bought of light snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred early Tuesday morning into Tuesday afternoon ahead of an approaching warm front, with a second heavier round of freezing rain and sleet for the region Tuesday Night into early Wednesday afternoon. This later episode caused numerous accidents and road closures.||Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet fell across Northeastern New Jersey during this 2 day storm, with 1/4 to 6/10ths of an inch of additional ice accretion.

Freezing rain fell across central and southern New Jersey overnight on February 1st. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch with the highest accretions in Monmouth County. The freezing rain started in the evening on February 1st. As warmer air moved in overnight, the freezing rain changed to plain rain around 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 2nd. Untreated roadways were hazardous. About 36,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state, but most of these outages occurred in west central and northern New Jersey. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and New York City was disrupted on the 2nd because of icy conditions. Many schools had delayed openings. All New Jersey Department of Motor Vehicle offices were closed at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd because of power outages. Some recycling and trash collections were delayed. This latest winter storm further taxed snow removal budgets, snow days on school calendars and exacerbated the shortage of salt. The onshore flow coupled with the higher astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused pockets of minor tidal flooding along the southeast coast of New Jersey during the early morning high tide cycle on the 2nd. The high tide reached 6.00 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) which is the threshold for minor tidal flooding. The high tide reached 6.73 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||The freezing rain was caused by a combination of a low pressure system that was moving east up the Ohio Valley and a high pressure system that cut across southern Canada. The former provided the rain while the latter helped leave enough cold air in place for freezing to occur overnight on the 1st. Enough warm air moved in at the surface close to sunrise on the 2nd to change the freezing rain to rain.

The combination of the strong west to northwest winds, low humidity levels and recent dry weather helped cause the rapid spread of wildfires across New Jersey during the day on the 19th. In all, ten wildfires, were reported across the state. The largest and most stubborn wildfire started about 530 a.m. EST in a mulch pile near the Reliable Wood Products on Broadway Road in South Brunswick Township in Middlesex County. By the time firefighters arrived, three piles of mulch were on fire with flames up to thirty feet high. It took fifty-eight fire fighters from three counties and the New Jersey Forestry Service to extinguish the blaze at around 930 a.m. EST on the 19th. In West Tuckerton (Ocean County), a wildfire consumed twenty acres before it was contained during the late afternoon of the 19th next to the Atlantis Golf Club. In Manalapan (Monmouth County), a brush fire reached 200 yards in length on Smithburg Road before it was contained. In Dennis Township (Cape May County), it took six fire departments two hours to contain a wildfire that started near a shed (destroyed it) and spread into nearby brush. Other wildfires were reported in Sayreville and Old Bridge in Monmouth County, Vineland in Cumberland County and Gloucester Township in Camden County.
The excessive weight from snow and ice caused a roof collapse in Verona New Jersey on February 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof of three connected garages at the Bradford Arms Apartments on East Bradford Avenue in Verona collapsed under the weight of snow and ice on Saturday February 5th. Two unoccupied vehicles were heavily damaged as a result of the collapse.

For the second time within a week heavy rain fell across New Jersey, especially the northern half. An additional 2 to 5 inches of precipitation fell and caused continued flooding in the Passaic Basin and renewed flooding on the Delaware and Raritan Rivers. Snowmelt in the upper reaches of the Delaware Basin contributed to the flooding. Major flooding occurred within the Passaic Basin, mostly minor flooding occurred on the Delaware River and in the Raritan Basin. Governor Chris Christie declared a state of emergency before the start of the second round of heavy rain on March 9th.  Throughout the state, 683 dwellings were affected by both flooding events and 207 suffered at least major damage. The second round of rain started during the early morning of the 10th, fell heavy at times during the daylight hours and evening on the 10th and ended between 4 a.m. and 8 a.m. EST on the morning on the 11th. Eastern Daylight Time started on the morning of the 13th. Some of the rivers began flooding during Eastern Standard Time and receded after Eastern Daylight Time began.||South of Trenton on the Delaware River, the combination of the up the Delaware Bay and River wind flow and freshwater runoff produced minor tidal flooding along the river and tidal sections of its tributaries during the late afternoon high tide cycle on the 10th and the early morning high tide cycle on the 11th. The highest tide occurred on the 11th and reached 8.93 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.20 feet above mean lower low water.||The heavy rain was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Southern Plains on the morning of the 8th northeast into Iowa on the morning of the 9th, Michigan on the morning of the 10th and into the eastern Great Lakes by the morning of the 11th. This system was able to tap moisture from both the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean and lift it over its frontal boundaries. The deep southeast flow was able to enhance the precipitation further over the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey. The rain ended once the low's occluded front moved through New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Delaware River at Trenton was above its 20 foot flood stage from 1015 p.m. EST on the 11th through 403 p.m. EST on the 12th. It crested at 20.59 feet at 730 a.m. EST on the 12th. Event precipitation totals within the county included 1.66 inches in Lawrence, 1.54 inches in Pennington, 1.50 inches in Ewing, 1.37 inches in Trenton and 1.26 inches in Princeton.

Strong to high west to northwest winds affected New Jersey from the evening of the 18th into the evening of the 19th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 55 mph. The winds tore down trees, tree limbs and wires and caused  power outages. Most of the highest winds and damage occurred in the central and southern part of the state. About 22,000 homes and businesses lost power. While more than two-thirds of the outages were resolved by the evening of the 19th, full power restoration did not occur until the evening of the 20th. All Delaware River Bridges in the local Philadelphia area had speed reductions and banned empty tractor-trailers through the evening of the 19th. The PATCO high speed commuter rail line into Philadelphia was shut down during the morning because of a power outage. The stronger winds began following a cold frontal passage on the evening of the 18th. The strong to high winds reached their peak speeds during the late morning and afternoon of the 19th and then slowly decreased during the evening of the 19th. | |In Middlesex County, three poles were snapped by the high winds on U.S. Route 1 in Plainsboro and closed two northbound lanes into the evening. Uprooted trees damaged a couple of homes and vehicles. In Mercer County, a partial scaffolding collapse on a new overpass on Interstate 195 in Robbinsville near the New Jersey Turnpike western extension closed a traffic lane for several hours during the afternoon. Two telephone poles snapped in Robbinsville on Tindall Road. In Burlington County, a downed century old oak tree damaged the back of a home in Cinnaminson Township. In Edgewater Park, a greater than 200-year-old white pine tree was split in half. In Camden County, Gloucester Township reported about fourteen weather related incidences. Large trees were reported knocked down in Haddon and Cherry Hill Townships. In Salem County, four roadways were closed because of downed trees. Speed limit signs were also ripped down on Golfwood Avenue in Carneys Point Township. In Cumberland County, a downed tree damaged the nursery room and attic of an unoccupied home on Cambridge Street in Vineland. The combination of the strong west to northwest winds, low humidity levels and recent dry weather helped cause the rapid spread of wildfires across New Jersey during the day on the 19th. In all, ten wildfires, were reported across the state. The largest and most stubborn wildfire started about 530 a.m. EST in a mulch pile near the Reliable Wood Products on Broadway Road in South Brunswick Township in Middlesex County. By the time firefighters arrived, three piles of mulch were on fire with flames up to thirty feet high. It took fifty-eight fire fighters from three counties and the New Jersey Forestry Service to extinguish the blaze at around 930 a.m. EST on the 19th. In West Tuckerton (Ocean County), a wildfire consumed twenty acres before it was contained during the late afternoon of the 19th next to the Atlantis Golf Club. In Manalapan (Monmouth County), a brush fire reached 200 yards in length on Smithburg Road before it was contained. In Dennis Township (Cape May County), it took six fire departments two hours to contain a wildfire that started near a shed (destroyed it) and spread into nearby brush. Other wildfires were reported in Sayreville and Old Bridge in Monmouth County, Vineland in Cumberland County and Gloucester Township in Camden County.

Freezing rain fell across central and southern New Jersey overnight on February 1st. Ice accretions were one tenth of an inch to one quarter of an inch with the highest accretions in Monmouth County. The freezing rain started in the evening on February 1st. As warmer air moved in overnight, the freezing rain changed to plain rain around 6 a.m. EST on the morning of the 2nd. Untreated roadways were hazardous. About 36,000 homes and businesses lost power in the state, but most of these outages occurred in west central and northern New Jersey. Amtrak rail service between Philadelphia and New York City was disrupted on the 2nd because of icy conditions. Many schools had delayed openings. All New Jersey Department of Motor Vehicle offices were closed at 1 p.m. EST on the 2nd because of power outages. Some recycling and trash collections were delayed. This latest winter storm further taxed snow removal budgets, snow days on school calendars and exacerbated the shortage of salt. The onshore flow coupled with the higher astronomical tides associated with the new moon caused pockets of minor tidal flooding along the southeast coast of New Jersey during the early morning high tide cycle on the 2nd. The high tide reached 6.00 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) which is the threshold for minor tidal flooding. The high tide reached 6.73 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||The freezing rain was caused by a combination of a low pressure system that was moving east up the Ohio Valley and a high pressure system that cut across southern Canada. The former provided the rain while the latter helped leave enough cold air in place for freezing to occur overnight on the 1st. Enough warm air moved in at the surface close to sunrise on the 2nd to change the freezing rain to rain.

Strong west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the afternoon and evening of the 14th in New Jersey. Peak wind gusts averaged close to 50 mph and tore down weak tree limbs and wires. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm partially tore off the roof of a building on Sherman Avenue in Seaside Heights. Shingles were blown off a few homes and a fence was also blown down around the Public Works Building on Sherman Avenue. A few homes and vehicles in the area were damaged by the flying debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex low pressure system tracked northeast from the Lower Mississippi River Valley into the Ohio Valley Tuesday, February 1st into Tuesday Night, with a secondary low pressure developing over the Delmarva region and tracking southeast of Long Island on Wednesday February 2nd.  With a polar high locked in place over Ontario and Quebec, this slow moving storm system brought a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region.  A bought of light snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred early Tuesday morning into Tuesday afternoon ahead of an approaching warm front, with a second heavier round of freezing rain and sleet for the region Tuesday Night into early Wednesday afternoon. This later episode caused numerous accidents and road closures.||Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet fell across Northeastern New Jersey during this 2 day storm, with 1/4 to 6/10ths of an inch of additional ice accretion.

About 22,000 homes and businesses lost power. While more than two-thirds of the outages were resolved by the evening of the 19th, full power restoration did not occur until the evening of the 20th. All Delaware River Bridges in the local Philadelphia area had speed reductions and banned empty tractor-trailers through the evening of the 19th. The PATCO high speed commuter rail line into Philadelphia was shut down during the morning because of a power outage. The stronger winds began following a cold frontal passage on the evening of the 18th. The strong to high winds reached their peak speeds during the late morning and afternoon of the 19th and then slowly decreased during the evening of the 19th. | |In Middlesex County, three poles were snapped by the high winds on U.S. Route 1 in Plainsboro and closed two northbound lanes into the evening. Uprooted trees damaged a couple of homes and vehicles. In Mercer County, a partial scaffolding collapse on a new overpass on Interstate 195 in Robbinsville near the New Jersey Turnpike western extension closed a traffic lane for several hours during the afternoon. Two telephone poles snapped in Robbinsville on Tindall Road. In Burlington County, a downed century old oak tree damaged the back of a home in Cinnaminson Township. In Edgewater Park, a greater than 200-year-old white pine tree was split in half. In Camden County, Gloucester Township reported about fourteen weather related incidences. Large trees were reported knocked down in Haddon and Cherry Hill Townships. In Salem County, four roadways were closed because of downed trees. Speed limit signs were also ripped down on Golfwood Avenue in Carneys Point Township. In Cumberland County, a downed tree damaged the nursery room and attic of an unoccupied home on Cambridge Street in Vineland. This was one of six wind related incidents in the city. ||Peak wind gusts included 59 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 58 mph in West Creek (Ocean County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 56 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 54 mph in Cream Ridge and Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), Ewing (Mercer County), Florence (Burlington County) and Hillsborough (Somerset County), 53 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County), Barnegat (Ocean County), Stewartsville (Warren County) and Perth Amboy (Middlesex County), 52 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport and Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 51 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), Ocean City (Cape May County), Millville (Cumberland County), Trenton (Mercer County) and Lumberton (Burlington County), 50 mph in Ringoes (Hunterdon County), 48 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), Kingwood (Hunterdon County), Somerville (Somerset Airport) and Chatsworth (Burlington County) and 47 mph in Blue Anchor (Camden County).||The strong to high winds were caused by the large pressure difference between a strong high pressure system building into the United States from central Canada and a low pressure system in the Canadian Maritimes. Winds started decreasing when the low pressure system moved east of the Canadian Maritimes on the night of the 19th and the high pressure system reached the Great Lakes.

A winter storm dropped 3 to 6 inches of snow across most of the southern half of New Jersey from the evening of the 21st into the early morning of the 22nd. Little if any snow fell north of Interstate 195 in New Jersey.  The snow began during the evening of the 21st, fell at its heaviest around Midnight EST on the 22nd and ended around 6 a.m EST on the 22nd. The morning commute was slowed by the overnight snow. Many school districts in the southern half of the state had either delayed openings or closings. Minor accidents were reported on state roadways, but no injuries were reported.||Representative snowfall included 6.0 inches in National Park (Gloucester County), 5.5 inches in Malaga (Gloucester County), 5.3 inches in Pittsgrove Township (Salem County), 5.0 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County) and Sewell (Gloucester County), 4.9 inches in Lindenwold (Camden County), 4.8 inches in North Wildwood (Cape May County), 4.7 inches in Maple Shade (Burlington County),  4.5 inches in  Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Newport (Cumberland County), 4.0 inches in Delran (Burlington County), Vineland (Cumberland County) and Ocean City (Cape May County), 3.5 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport and 2.8 inches in Florence (Burlington County). The onshore flow coupled with the higher astronomical tides associated with the recent full moon caused minor tidal flooding along the southeast coast of New Jersey during the late evening high tide on the 21st. The high tide reached 6.28 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.0 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide reached 7.38 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. ||The winter storm was caused by the second in a series of low pressure systems that moved quickly west to east across the United States. This low pressure system moved eastsoutheast from Illinois on the morning of the 21st to near Cincinnati, Ohio at 7 p.m. EST on the 21st, near Raleigh, North Carolina at 1 a.m. EST on the 22nd and then sank south into South Carolina later that morning, The general fast motion and direction of movement kept snowfall totals in the single numbers and also prevented much snow from falling in northern New Jersey.

Strong west winds followed a cold frontal passage during the afternoon and evening of the 14th in New Jersey. Peak wind gusts averaged close to 50 mph and tore down weak tree limbs and wires.  In Cumberland County, in Vineland, the strong wind tore off a ten foot by forty foot section of an old facade from a vacant downtown business building on East Landis Avenue. The building has been unoccupied since at least 2005. Peak wind gusts included 53 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 52 mph in Pert Amboy (Middlesex County) and Brick (Ocean County), 50 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and Barnegat (Ocean County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County) and Hackettstown (Warren County), 47 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 46 mph in Lumberton (Burlington County), Morristown (Morris County) and Millville (Cumberland County). The strong winds were fueled by the pressure difference between the cold front's low pressure system that moved through New England and a high pressure system building east from the western Great Lakes. The strong winds slowly decreased overnight as the low pressure system moved farther away from New Jersey.

The runoff from the combination of rain and melting snow caused isolated minor river flooding in Somerset and Morris Counties from the late afternoon of the 25th into the evening of the 26th. Rain started on the evening of the 24th and fell at a fairly steady rate during the calendar day on the 25th until a cold front moved through the counties during the early afternoon on the 25th. Event precipitation totals averaged 1.0 to 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 340 p.m. EST through 641 p.m. EST on the 25th. It crested at 2.56 feet at 530 p.m. EST. Event precipitation totals included 1.53 inches in Boonton, 1.39 inches in Chatham and Long Hill and 1.28 inches in Parsippany.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the combination of rain and melting snow caused isolated minor river flooding in Somerset and Morris Counties from the late afternoon of the 25th into the evening of the 26th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Branch of the Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 456 p.m.EST through 549 p.m. EST on the 25th. It crested at 7.1 feet at 545 p.m. EST. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 655 p.m. EST on the 25th through 841 p.m. EST on the 26th. It crested at 10.83 feet at 7 a.m. EST on the 26th.||Event precipitation totals included 1.56 inches in Bernards Township, 1.42 inches in Pottersville, 1.27 inches in Montgomery Township, 1.24 inches in Franklin Township, 1.20 inches in Somerville and Bound Brook, 1.16 inches in Neshanic and Skillman, 1.15 inches in Bridgewater Township and Belle Mead and 1.13 inches in Hillsborough Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong cold frontal passage produced strong to high winds across New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening on the 25th. Peak wind gusts averaged 50 to 60 mph, with most of the highest gusts in the southern half of the state. The winds downed numerous trees, tree limbs and power lines and also caused some structural damage.  Strong south winds started during the mid to late morning of the 25th, but by far the highest wind gusts occurred during the first hour after the cold front passed that afternoon. Lesser, but still strong, wind gusts persisted into the evening. ||In Atlantic City (Atlantic County), an eight block stretch of the Boardwalk was closed after the high winds tore down a billboard atop of the Central Pier and sent plywood onto the street. In Atlantic County, about 2,000 homes and businesses lost power in Northfield, Linwood and Somers Point. In Cumberland County, about 3,200 homes and businesses lost power. In Salem County, a tree fell onto and damaged a garage in Woodstown. In Camden County, poles were snapped in Pine Hill and fencing was blown down in Erial. ||Peak wind gusts included 61 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 60 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 59 mph in Brick (Ocean County), 57 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 55 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County), Clayton (Gloucester County) and Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 54 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 52 mph in Trenton (Mercer County), 51 mph in Cream Ridge (Monmouth County).||The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure difference between a large high pressure system in the middle of the United States and an intense low pressure system that moved through eastern New England and the Canadian Maritimes. The winds received an additional boost from the large atmospheric pressure rises that occurred just after the cold front passed.

A complex low pressure system tracked northeast from the Lower Mississippi River Valley into the Ohio Valley Tuesday, February 1st into Tuesday Night, with a secondary low pressure developing over the Delmarva region and tracking southeast of Long Island on Wednesday February 2nd.  With a polar high locked in place over Ontario and Quebec, this slow moving storm system brought a mixture of snow, sleet and freezing rain to the region.  A bought of light snow, sleet and freezing rain occurred early Tuesday morning into Tuesday afternoon ahead of an approaching warm front, with a second heavier round of freezing rain and sleet for the region Tuesday Night into early Wednesday afternoon. This later episode caused numerous accidents and road closures.||Generally 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet fell across Northeastern New Jersey during this 2 day storm, with 1/4 to 6/10ths of an inch of additional ice accretion.

A weak low pressure system tracking along a warm front south of the region Sunday night into Monday morning on February 21st brought a moderate to heavy snowfall to the region.||Generally 3 to 5 inches of snow fell across southern portions of the region, while across northern portions of Northeastern New Jersey, a heavier band brought snowfall amounts of 5 to 8 inches.

Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in New Jersey and stream and river flooding mainly in west central and northern New Jersey. Major flooding occurred again in sections of the Passaic River Basin.  The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 2.0  to 4.0 inches along the Interstate 95 corridor and Morris County and generally 1 to 2 inches elsewhere in New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding along Parkers Creek in Mount Laurel Township forced the closure of Union Mill Road. The heavy rain also caused basement flooding of homes on Oregon Trail in Medford Township. Event precipitation totals included 2.89 inches in Mount Laurel, 2.79 inches in Lumberton, 2.78 inches in Westampton, 2.61 inches in Medford, 2.50 inches in Wrightstown, 2.46 inches in Moorestown, 2.43 inches in Southampton, 2.33 inches in  Medford Lakes and 2.32 inches in Burlington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to high southeast to south winds affected central and southern New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 to 55 mph with some isolated wind gusts to around 60 mph. The highest wind gusts occurred during the evening and the worst reported wind damage occurred in Cumberland County. The strong to high winds coupled with the heavy rain, knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. The strong to high gradient winds were exacerbated further by isolated severe thunderstorms. About 18,000 homes and businesses lost power in New Jersey, all power was restored by the night of the 17th. In Cumberland County, in Millville, the high winds snapped a pole that held a bank of lights used to illuminate the Ewan Field baseball diamond. In Gandys Beach in Downe Township, the high winds knocked down poles and carried away a shed. ||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 56 mph in Ocean City (Cape May County) and Cream Ridge (Monmouth County), 53 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 52 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 48 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 47 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 46 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County) and at the McGuire Air Force Base. The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system departing the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley. Winds eased after the occluded front moved through New Jersey late in the evening on the 16th.

Strong south winds preceded the passage of a cold front across southeastern New Jersey during the midday on the 28th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down tree limbs and some weak trees and wires. Peak wind gusts included 49 mph at both the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Tuckerton (Ocean County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Ramapo Valley Rd. and North Ramapo Ave. was closed due to flooding in West Mahwah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure tracked from the Great Lakes into Southeast Canada, a triple point low pressure system developed in the Ohio Valley.  This storm system brought flooding rains to portions of Northeast New Jersey as it tracked north and west of the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 46 was flooded between Andrew Dr. and McBride Ave. in Woodland Park. Automobile water rescues were performed by the local fire department.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of low pressure tracked from the Great Lakes into Southeast Canada, a triple point low pressure system developed in the Ohio Valley.  This storm system brought flooding rains to portions of Northeast New Jersey as it tracked north and west of the local area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Ridgewood, Linwood Ave. between North Maple Ave. and Fairfield Ave. and the intersection of Irving St. and Franklin Ave. were closed due to flooding of the HoHoKus Brook.  In Saddle River, Rochelle Ave. between Essex St. and Becker St. was flooded from the Saddle River.  Wageraw Rd. south of Lincoln Ave. was closed due to flooding of the Passaic River. The HoHoKus Brook at HoHoKus was above its flood stage of 3.0 ft. between 19:30 on April 16 and 08:30 on April 17 and crested at 4.83 ft. at 22:30 on April 16.  The Saddle River at Saddle was above its flood stage of 4.5 ft. between 20:30 on April 16 and 01:45 on April 18 and crested at 5.02 ft. at 22:45 on April 16.

Peak wind gusts were mostly between 45 and 50 mph and included 49 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 47 mph in Monmouth Beach (Monmouth County) and Brick Township (Ocean County) and 44 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County) and Perth Amboy (Middlesex County).

Snow fell over the higher terrain of Sussex and Warren Counties during the morning of April 1st. Most of the accumulations (on average 1 to 3 inches) were on grassy areas. Precipitation started as rain on the 31st of March, but changed over to snow as colder air moved in behind the departure and intensification of a second low pressure system. Even though snow persisted into the early afternoon on the 1st, it had a hard time accumulating even on grassy surfaces as the morning progressed. ||Representative snowfall included 4.2 inches in High Point (Sussex County), 2.5 inches in Hardwick (Warren County), 2.2 inches in Wantage (Sussex County) and 1.4 inches in Greenwich and Stewartsville (Warren County).  ||The snow was caused by the second in a pair of low pressure systems that formed over the southeastern United States. The first one reached the Delmarva coastal waters during the early evening of March 31st and weakened as the second low pressure system exited the Georgia coast. At 2 a.m. EDT on April 1st, the second low pressure system reached the North Carolina coastal waters and had become the stronger of the two lows. It continued to intensify and move northeast and at 8 a.m. EDT on April 1st, it reached Cape Cod Massachusetts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter observed three funnel clouds about 2 miles to the north-northwest of Wayne.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds affected extreme southeast New Jersey preceding a cold front on the morning of the 5th. After the cold front moved through the state, strong northwest winds affected the higher terrain in the northern half of the state and most of southern New Jersey from the late morning through the first half of the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down tree limbs, some weak trees and wires. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 52 mph in Ocean City (Cape May County), 51 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 49 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 48 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County) and 44 mph in Red Lion (Burlington County).

Showers and thunderstorms preceding a strong cold frontal passage exacerbated the synoptic scale wind pattern and produced isolated pockets of damaging winds across extreme southwest New Jersey during the morning of the 5th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and power lines in western Salem County in and around Carney's Point Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong southeast onshore flow on the 16th combined with the already high astronomical tides associated with the full moon to produce minor to moderate tidal flooding along the New Jersey oceanic coast and moderate to severe flooding in Delaware Bay in Cape May and Cumberland Counties. Tidal flooding departures increased farther up both Delaware and Raritan Bays as the high tide cycle coincided closer to the strongest winds. In addition, the funneling effect of southeast winds up the Delaware Bay contributed to increasing tidal departures. The high tide at Reedy Point (New Castle County Delaware) established an all-time record high.||In Cumberland County, the tidal flooding was described as the worst in 10 to 20 years. Maurice River, Commerical and Downe Townships were hardest hit. Many people either became trapped in their homes or vehicles. One woman was injured. Flooding was reported at Gandy's Beach and Money Island (Downe Township),  Port Norris in Commercial Township and along the Maurice River between Commerical and Maurice River Township. ||In Downe Township, in Gandy beach, a woman suffered a cut after a wave struck her home and broke a window. Every homeowner on Cove Road had bulkhead damage and there was a 75 to 100 foot bulkhead breach on North Cove Road. Access was cut off to about 12 to 15 homes on South Cove Road after roadway damage. The road was reopened on the 17th. In Fortescue, a one mile stretch of the roadway was covered with up to four feet of water. In Commerical Township, the tide backed up the Maurice River. The State Police station on Ogden Avenue was flooded. The tide washed out the docks and destroyed a parking lot. Shell Pile Road was rendered impassable. A forty foot long dike within the township was badly damaged. Farther up the Maurice River, flooding occurred along Mauricetown Road in Mauricetown.| |The high tide at Reedy Point (New Castle County) reached 9.26 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide on record. Severe flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. Farther down Delaware Bay at Bivalve in Cumberland County, the high tide reached 8.55 feet above mean lower low water. There is no established threshold for flood categories at this site. At the Cape May Ferry Terminal, the high tide reached 7.72 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. Along the ocean side, the high tide reached 7.07 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May Harbor (Cape May County) and 6.89 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Both were in the minor tidal flooding category.  Moderate tidal flooding occurred around Raritan Bay and in Monmouth County.  At Sandy Hook, the high tide reached 8.02 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide at South Amboy (Middlesex County) reached 8.99 feet above mean lower low water.

The strong southeast wind up the Delaware Bay and River combined with the already high astronomical tides associated with the full moon and fresh water runoff from heavy rain to produce severe tidal flooding along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries during the evening and overnight high tide cycle on the 16th. The high tide at Reedy Point in Delaware established an all-time record high and the high tide in Philadelphia tied its all-time record high first established on November 25, 1950.||While tidal flooding affected all the counties along the Delaware River from Burlington south through Salem, the worst reported damage was in Gloucester and Salem Counties. In Salem County, the tidal flooding was reported to be the worst in over a decade.||In Salem County, the hardest hit communities included Carneys Point, Elsinboro and Pennsville. In Elsinboro Township, about twenty homes were surrounded by flood waters, but suffered no damage. A nursing home though was evacuated after flood waters entered the front door. Most of the remaining damage occurred to roadways. Sinnickson Landing Road (County Route 661) was open to only emergency personnel. Hook Road (County Route 551) had its west side severely undermined, but remained open through repairs. In Mannington Township, Pointers-Auburn Road (County Route 540) was closed over the Mannington Creek until late on the 17th after the east side of the roadway was undermined. Flood waters from the Salem Creek poured over New Jersey State Route 49 in Pennsville Township. In Gloucester County, forty homes and seven businesses suffered major damage and another forty homes suffered minor damage as flooding occurred along the Woodbury, Mantua, Big Timber and Repaupo Creeks. These creeks all empty into the Delaware River. Homes were damaged in West Deptford (Woodbury and Mantua Creeks) Westville (Southwest Branch of the Big Timber Creek), Woodbury (Woodbury Creek) and Logan (Repaupo Creek). ||In Camden County, in Camden City, a man drove into the Cooper River near the 10th Street Bridge and drowned. Tidal related flooding extended as far inland as Haddon Township. The Cooper River flooded the Admiral Wilson Boulevard (U.S. Route 30) in Camden and Pennsauken. It also flooded both the North and South Park Drives between Collingswood and Pennsauken. Flooding along South Park Drive extended into Haddon Township. The Cooper River also engulfed the parking lot of the Cooper River Yacht Club in Collinswood. The Camden Golf Academy's driving range in Pennsauken was flooded. In Burlington County, the Delaware River flooded some streets around North Chester Avenue in Delran. Basements were flooded from the combination of the river flooding and high water tables. In Burlington City, the Delaware River backed into the city's drainage system. Wood Street was closed because of flooding near Delaware Avenue West. ||The high tide at Reedy Point (Delaware) reached 9.26 feet above mean lower low water and established a new record. Severe tidal flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water, At Philadelphia Pier 12, the high tide reached 10.49 feet above mean lower low water at 1254 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Severe tidal flooding begins at 10.2 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide reached 11.96 feet above mean lower low water in Burlington City (Burlington County) and 10.04 feet above mean lower low water at Palmyra (Burlington County). There are no established tidal reference categories at these latter two sites. The strong southeast winds persisted and reached its peak as the high tide was working its way up the Delaware Bay and Delaware River. The surface winds did not shift to the west until just before the high tide occurred in Philadelphia. Minor tidal flooding also occurred during the overnight high tide cycles on the 17th, 18th and 19th, but not on the same magnitude as the 16th.

Strong to high southeast to south winds affected central and southern New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 to 55 mph with some isolated wind gusts to around 60 mph. The highest wind gusts occurred during the evening and the worst reported wind damage occurred in Cumberland County. The strong to high winds coupled with the heavy rain, knocked down weak trees, tree limbs and wires. The strong to high gradient winds were exacerbated further by isolated severe thunderstorms. About 18,000 homes and businesses lost power in New Jersey, all power was restored by the night of the 17th. In Cumberland County, in Millville, the high winds snapped a pole that held a bank of lights used to illuminate the Ewan Field baseball diamond. In Gandys Beach in Downe Township, the high winds knocked down poles and carried away a shed. ||Peak wind gusts included 58 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 56 mph in Ocean City (Cape May County) and Cream Ridge (Monmouth County), 53 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 52 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County), 48 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 47 mph in Harvey Cedars (Ocean County), 46 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 45 mph in Perth Amboy (Middlesex County) and at the McGuire Air Force Base. The strong to high winds were caused by the pressure gradient (difference) between a strong high pressure system departing the Canadian Maritimes and an approaching cold front from the Ohio Valley. Winds eased after the occluded front moved through New Jersey late in the evening on the 16th.

The strong southeast onshore flow on the 16th combined with the already high astronomical tides associated with the full moon to produce minor to moderate tidal flooding along the New Jersey oceanic coast and moderate to severe flooding in Delaware Bay in Cape May and Cumberland Counties. Tidal flooding departures increased farther up both Delaware and Raritan Bays as the high tide cycle coincided closer to the strongest winds. In addition, the funneling effect of southeast winds up the Delaware Bay contributed to increasing tidal departures. The high tide at Reedy Point (New Castle County Delaware) established an all-time record high.||In Cumberland County, the tidal flooding was described as the worst in 10 to 20 years. Maurice River, Commerical and Downe Townships were hardest hit. Many people either became trapped in their homes or vehicles. One woman was injured. Flooding was reported at Gandy's Beach and Money Island (Downe Township),  Port Norris in Commercial Township and along the Maurice River between Commerical and Maurice River Township. ||In Downe Township, in Gandy beach, a woman suffered a cut after a wave struck her home and broke a window. Every homeowner on Cove Road had bulkhead damage and there was a 75 to 100 foot bulkhead breach on North Cove Road. Access was cut off to about 12 to 15 homes on South Cove Road after roadway damage. The road was reopened on the 17th. In Fortescue, a one mile stretch of the roadway was covered with up to four feet of water. In Commerical Township, the tide backed up the Maurice River. The State Police station on Ogden Avenue was flooded. The tide washed out the docks and destroyed a parking lot. Shell Pile Road was rendered impassable. A forty foot long dike within the township was badly damaged. Farther up the Maurice River, flooding occurred along Mauricetown Road in Mauricetown.| |The high tide at Reedy Point (New Castle County) reached 9.26 feet above mean lower low water. This was the highest tide on record. Severe flooding starts at 9.2 feet above mean lower low water. Farther down Delaware Bay at Bivalve in Cumberland County, the high tide reached 8.55 feet above mean lower low water. There is no established threshold for flood categories at this site. At the Cape May Ferry Terminal, the high tide reached 7.72 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. Along the ocean side, the high tide reached 7.07 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May Harbor (Cape May County) and 6.89 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Both were in the minor tidal flooding category.  Moderate tidal flooding occurred around Raritan Bay and in Monmouth County.  At Sandy Hook, the high tide reached 8.02 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide at South Amboy (Middlesex County) reached 8.99 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm air mass for late April helped trigger some widely scattered strong to severe thunderstorms in New Jersey during the afternoon of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in central Morris County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds affected extreme southeast New Jersey preceding a cold front on the morning of the 5th. After the cold front moved through the state, strong northwest winds affected the higher terrain in the northern half of the state and most of southern New Jersey from the late morning through the first half of the afternoon. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph and knocked down tree limbs, some weak trees and wires. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 52 mph in Ocean City (Cape May County), 51 mph in Tuckerton (Ocean County), 49 mph at High Point (Sussex County), 48 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County) and 44 mph in Red Lion (Burlington County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped trigger thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 19th. The thunderstorms were training and not fast movers. Coupled with the recent run of wet weather helped cause flash flooding in northwestern Warren County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 2 to 4 inches where repeat (training) thunderstorms occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage and small creek flash flooding in northwestern Warren County mainly in Hardwick and Blairstown Townships. Event precipitation totals within Warren County included 2.35 inches in Philipsburg and 1.82 in Washington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in New Jersey and stream and river flooding mainly in west central and northern New Jersey. Major flooding occurred again in sections of the Passaic River Basin.  The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 2.0  to 4.0 inches along the Interstate 95 corridor and Morris County and generally 1 to 2 inches elsewhere in New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage and river flooding in Morris County. Moderate to major flooding occurred along the Passaic and Pompton Rivers, although the crests were not as high as they were in March. The renewed flooding along the Pompton and Passaic Rivers forced the evacuations of 10 residents in Lincoln Park and three families in Pequannock. About 100 homeowners in Pequannock were told to evacuate. About 30 homes were damaged by the Pompton River within the township. New Jersey State Route 23 was also closed in Pequannock.||The Passaic River at Chatham was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1028 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 322 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 6.34 feet at 100 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Farther downstream, the Passaic River at Pine Brook was above its 19 foot flood stage from 500 p.m. EDT on the 17th through 915 p.m. EDT on the 20th. It crested at 20.09 feet at 1245 a.m. EDT on the 19th. Farther downstream, the Passaic River at Circle Burners or Lincoln Park was above its 6 foot flood stage from about 2 p.m. EDT on the 18th through 5 a.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 8.33 feet at 1140 p.m. EDT on the 18th. ||The Pompton River at Pompton Plains was above its 16 foot flood stage from 207 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 535 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 19.84 feet at 130 p.m.EDT on the 17th. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at Ryerson Road was above its 12 foot flood stage from 357 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 917 p.m. EDT on the 20th. It crested at 16.41 feet at 1020 p.m. EDT on the 17th. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at Midwood was above its 7.5 foot flood stage from 335 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 122 a.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 12.40 feet at 1205 a.m. EDT on the 18th.  Farther downstream, the Pompton River at the Mountainview Bridge was above its 8 foot flood stage from 1017 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 921 a.m. EDT on the 21st. It crested at 10.84 feet at 250 a.m. EDT on the 18th. | |The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 840 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 823 p.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 4.18 feet at 450 a.m. EDT on the 17th. The Rockaway River above the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 251 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 825 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 6.23 feet at 11 a.m. EDT on the 17th. The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 618 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 222 a.m. EDT on the 19th. It crested at 6.75 feet at 430 p.m. EDT on the 17th. The Whippany Creek at Morristown was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1208 a.m. EDT through 707 am EDT on the 17th. It crested at 6.2 feet at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 17th. The Pequannock River at the Macopin Dam was above its 5.5 foot flood stage from 1218 a.m. EDT on the 17th through 1007 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 5.73 feet at 745 a.m. EDT on the 17th. ||Event precipitation totals included 3.82 inches in Butler, 3.81 inches in Milton, 3.60 inches in Rockaway, 3.26 inches in Morris, 3.14 inches in Chatham, 3.10 inches in Harding, 3.09 inches in Denville, 2.91 inches in Mine Hill and Randolph, 2.90 inches in Long Valley, 2.88 inches in Boonton and 2.84 inches in Long Hill.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike in Brielle downed some wires near Agnes Road and New Jersey State Route 71. About 500 homes and businesses lost power for six and a half hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms that rode along a warm frontal boundary during the evening of the 23rd moved through northwest New Jersey. They weakened as they crossed into New Jersey, but still produced wind damage in Warren County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in southeastern Warren County in and around Washington Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong to high northwest winds that occurred throughout the day caused blowout tides to occur along the New Jersey coast as well as in Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries during the late afternoon and evening low tide cycle on the 19th. New Jersey State Police had to borrow a hovercraft to rescue six people from a pontoon boat that became stuck on a mudflat in Risley Channel in Egg Harbor Township (Atlantic County). The low tide reached 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 1.90 feet below mean lower low water at Avalon (Cape May County), 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 2.10 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware, 1.91 feet below mean lower low water in Philadelphia, 1.89 feet below mean lower low water in Talcony (Burlington County) and 1.85 feet below mean lower low water in Burlington  (Burlington County). Blowout tides start at levels less than 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

The strong to high northwest winds that occurred throughout the day caused blowout tides to occur along the New Jersey coast as well as in Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries during the late afternoon and evening low tide cycle on the 19th. New Jersey State Police had to borrow a hovercraft to rescue six people from a pontoon boat that became stuck on a mudflat in Risley Channel in Egg Harbor Township (Atlantic County). The low tide reached 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 1.90 feet below mean lower low water at Avalon (Cape May County), 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 2.10 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware, 1.91 feet below mean lower low water in Philadelphia, 1.89 feet below mean lower low water in Talcony (Burlington County) and 1.85 feet below mean lower low water in Burlington  (Burlington County). Blowout tides start at levels less than 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

The same low pressure system that brought strong winds to central and southern New Jersey on the 27th, brought additional strong west to northwest winds to eastern parts of the state as well as the higher terrain in Sussex County during the daytime on the 28th. Peak wind gusts near the coast averaged 45 to 50 mph and brought down additional tree limbs and weak trees.  Peak wind gusts included 48 mph in Brick (Ocean County), 47 mph in Cape May (Cape May County) and Wantage (Sussex County) and 44 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). The second round of strong winds was caused by the strong sea level pressure difference between the departing but intensifying low pressure system as it moved northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Great Lakes. Once the high pressure system moved closer, the strong winds subsided during the late afternoon.

The same low pressure system that brought strong winds to central and southern New Jersey on the 27th, brought additional strong west to northwest winds to eastern parts of the state as well as the higher terrain in Sussex County during the daytime on the 28th. Peak wind gusts near the coast averaged 45 to 50 mph and brought down additional tree limbs and weak trees.  Peak wind gusts included 48 mph in Brick (Ocean County), 47 mph in Cape May (Cape May County) and Wantage (Sussex County) and 44 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). The second round of strong winds was caused by the strong sea level pressure difference between the departing but intensifying low pressure system as it moved northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Great Lakes. Once the high pressure system moved closer, the strong winds subsided during the late afternoon.

A rapidly intensifying low pressure system caused strong northwest winds to occur across Eastern New Jersey and over the higher terrain of Sussex County from the middle of the evening on the 7th through the early morning on the 8th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. Combined with the heavy rain, the strong winds downed tree limbs, shallow rooted trees and caused widely scattered power outages.||Peak wind gusts included 57 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 53 mph in West Cape May (Cape May County), 52 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 51 mph in Tuckerton and Brick (Ocean County), 50 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 49 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 46 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 43 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County). The strong winds were caused by the increasing circulation around the same low pressure system that brought heavy rain to the state. The low intensified from 1007 millibars over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th, to 996 millibars over Delaware and Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 7th, to 989 millibars over Long Island at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th to 978 millibars over Eastport, Maine at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. The 29 millibar drop in 24 hours qualified it for the meteorological definition of a bombing low pressure system and was the main reason for the strong winds. While the low continued to intensify (it was down to 963 millibars at the mouth of the Saint Lawrence River at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th), it moved far enough away from New Jersey so that the strong winds subsided after the sun rose on the 8th.

A rapidly intensifying low pressure system caused strong northwest winds to occur across Eastern New Jersey and over the higher terrain of Sussex County from the middle of the evening on the 7th through the early morning on the 8th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 50 mph. Combined with the heavy rain, the strong winds downed tree limbs, shallow rooted trees and caused widely scattered power outages.||Peak wind gusts included 57 mph in Cape May (Cape May County), 53 mph in West Cape May (Cape May County), 52 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 51 mph in Tuckerton and Brick (Ocean County), 50 mph in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 49 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and Belmar (Monmouth County), 46 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County) and 43 mph in Bivalve (Cumberland County). The strong winds were caused by the increasing circulation around the same low pressure system that brought heavy rain to the state. The low intensified from 1007 millibars over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th, to 996 millibars over Delaware and Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 7th, to 989 millibars over Long Island at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th to 978 millibars over Eastport, Maine at 7 a.m. EST on the 8th. The 29 millibar drop in 24 hours qualified it for the meteorological definition of a bombing low pressure system and was the main reason for the strong winds. While the low continued to intensify (it was down to 963 millibars at the mouth of the Saint Lawrence River at 1 p.m. EST on the 8th), it moved far enough away from New Jersey so that the strong winds subsided after the sun rose on the 8th.

Snow fell across the northern half of New Jersey from the evening of the 20th into the early morning of the 21st and a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell across southwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 21st. Mainly rain fell across southeast New Jersey. Snowfall averaged 1 to 2 inches in southwest New Jersey and 3 to 5 inches in the northern half of New Jersey. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch.||Snow began in far northwest New Jersey during the evening of the 20th and slowly spread to the south, beginning in the southwest part of the state shortly after Midnight EST on the 21st. Precipitation fell as rain in the southeast part of the state. During the early morning, precipitation mixed with sleet and freezing rain in the southwest part of the state. In the northern part of the state, the snow fell at its heaviest around 3 a.m. EST. The snow ended between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST. Its greatest impact was on the morning commute in the northern part of the state.||Representative snowfall included 4.8 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 4.5 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 4.2 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 4.0 inches in Butler (Morris County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 3.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), Belle Mead (Somerset County) and East Brunswick (Middlesex County), 3.0 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 2.5 inches in Yardville (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in Delran (Burlington County), 1.5 inches in Verga (Gloucester County) and 1.3 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County).||The snow and wintry mix were caused by a developing low pressure system that formed in the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon of the 20th and moved northeast. It reached West Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 20th, the lower Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 1 a.m. EST on the 21st, right over Atlantic City, New Jersey at 4 a.m. EST on the 21st and then accelerated eastward as it strengthened. At 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, the low was already southeast of Montauk Point, New York.

A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of northeast New Jersey. This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  Areas just north and west of New York City were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across interior northeast New Jersey, with up to 18 inches of snowfall across higher elevations.||Thousands of people across northeast New Jersey lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn. This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A significant number of trees came down across the county starting around 1 pm EST due to 3 to 6 inches of heavy wet snow.||In Union, a tree came down on a car across Route 22 East on the Garden State Parkway around 1 pm EST. In Mountainside, all lanes were closed on US 22 East at Mountain Avenue due to downed trees around 2 pm EST. In Springfield, multiple exits along I-78 were closed due to downed trees on the afternoon of 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong to high northwest winds that occurred throughout the day caused blowout tides to occur along the New Jersey coast as well as in Delaware Bay and tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries during the late afternoon and evening low tide cycle on the 19th. New Jersey State Police had to borrow a hovercraft to rescue six people from a pontoon boat that became stuck on a mudflat in Risley Channel in Egg Harbor Township (Atlantic County). The low tide reached 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 1.90 feet below mean lower low water at Avalon (Cape May County), 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 2.10 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware, 1.91 feet below mean lower low water in Philadelphia, 1.89 feet below mean lower low water in Talcony (Burlington County) and 1.85 feet below mean lower low water in Burlington  (Burlington County). Blowout tides start at levels less than 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.

Snow fell across the northern half of New Jersey from the evening of the 20th into the early morning of the 21st and a wintry mix of snow, sleet and freezing rain fell across southwest New Jersey during the early morning of the 21st. Mainly rain fell across southeast New Jersey. Snowfall averaged 1 to 2 inches in southwest New Jersey and 3 to 5 inches in the northern half of New Jersey. Ice accretions were less than one tenth of an inch.||Snow began in far northwest New Jersey during the evening of the 20th and slowly spread to the south, beginning in the southwest part of the state shortly after Midnight EST on the 21st. In the northern part of the state, the snow fell at its heaviest around 3 a.m. EST. The snow ended between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST. Its greatest impact was on the morning commute in the northern part of the state.||Representative snowfall included 4.8 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 4.5 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 4.2 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 4.0 inches in Butler (Morris County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 3.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), Belle Mead (Somerset County) and East Brunswick (Middlesex County), 3.0 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 2.5 inches in Yardville (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in Delran (Burlington County), 1.5 inches in Verga (Gloucester County) and 1.3 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County).||The snow and wintry mix were caused by a developing low pressure system that formed in the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon of the 20th and moved northeast. It reached West Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 20th, the lower Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 1 a.m. EST on the 21st, right over Atlantic City, New Jersey at 4 a.m. EST on the 21st and then accelerated eastward as it strengthened. At 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, the low was already southeast of Montauk Point, New York.

Precipitation fell as rain in the southeast part of the state. During the early morning, precipitation mixed with sleet and freezing rain in the southwest part of the state. In the northern part of the state, the snow fell at its heaviest around 3 a.m. EST. The snow ended between 5 a.m. EST and 7 a.m. EST. Its greatest impact was on the morning commute in the northern part of the state.||Representative snowfall included 4.8 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 4.5 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 4.2 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 4.0 inches in Butler (Morris County) and Wantage (Sussex County), 3.5 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), Belle Mead (Somerset County) and East Brunswick (Middlesex County), 3.0 inches in Freehold (Monmouth County), 2.5 inches in Yardville (Mercer County), 1.6 inches in Delran (Burlington County), 1.5 inches in Verga (Gloucester County) and 1.3 inches in Pennsauken (Camden County).||The snow and wintry mix were caused by a developing low pressure system that formed in the Tennessee Valley during the afternoon of the 20th and moved northeast. It reached West Virginia at 7 p.m. EST on the 20th, the lower Susquehanna Valley in Pennsylvania at 1 a.m. EST on the 21st, right over Atlantic City, New Jersey at 4 a.m. EST on the 21st and then accelerated eastward as it strengthened. At 7 a.m. EST on the 21st, the low was already southeast of Montauk Point, New York.

A protracted rain event from the daylight hours on the 6th through the early morning on the 8th culminated with heavy rain during the afternoon and evening of the 7th and caused poor drainage flooding in New Jersey. It also caused minor to locally moderate river and creek flooding in the Raritan and Passaic River Basins in northern New Jersey and minor creek and river flooding in southwest New Jersey. Flooding in the Passaic Basin persisted into the early morning of the 11th. Event precipitation totals averaged 2 to 3 inches. The rain was caused by waves of low pressure that moved northeast along a slowly moving frontal boundary. The cold front was in central Pennsylvania on the morning of the 6th and eastern Pennsylvania on the morning of the 7th. The last wave of low pressure on this boundary, located over western North Carolina at 7 a.m. EST on the 7th intensified rapidly as the 7th progress. It was during this intensification process that rain fell at its heaviest that afternoon and evening. The low pressure system itself passed through Delaware during the early evening of the 7th and was over the Monmouth County, New Jersey coastal waters at 10 p.m. EST on the 7th. It reached Long Island, New York at 1 a.m. EST on the 8th. Shortly after the low reached Long Island, the rain ended across New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 329 a.m. EST through 801 a.m. EST on the 8th. It crested at 2.92 feet at 600 a.m. EST on the 8th. Event precipitation totals included 2.43 inches in Winslow Township, 2.34 inches in Berlin Township and 1.96 inches in Merchantville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same low pressure system that brought strong winds to central and southern New Jersey on the 27th, brought additional strong west to northwest winds to eastern parts of the state as well as the higher terrain in Sussex County during the daytime on the 28th. Peak wind gusts near the coast averaged 45 to 50 mph and brought down additional tree limbs and weak trees.  Peak wind gusts included 48 mph in Brick (Ocean County), 47 mph in Cape May (Cape May County) and Wantage (Sussex County) and 44 mph at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). The second round of strong winds was caused by the strong sea level pressure difference between the departing but intensifying low pressure system as it moved northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Great Lakes. Once the high pressure system moved closer, the strong winds subsided during the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty west to northwest winds occurred across the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey following a cold frontal passage during the morning of the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 55 mph and tore down weak tree limbs. Peak wind gusts included 56 mph in Wantage (Sussex County) and 44 mph in Stewartsville (Warren County).

A historic and unprecedented early-season winter storm impacted the area on Saturday, October 29, with more than one foot of heavy wet snow falling on interior portions of northeast New Jersey. This is the first time a winter storm of this magnitude has ever occurred in October.||Late Friday night, October 28, an area of low pressure emerged from the South Carolina coastal plain into the western Atlantic Ocean.  The low moved up the coast late Friday night into Saturday morning.  The low began to rapidly strengthen late Saturday morning when it was east of the Delmarva coast, and continued to strengthen as it passed south of Long Island Saturday night.  Areas just north and west of New York City were just cold enough to support a heavy wet snow.  The heaviest snow fell across interior northeast New Jersey, with up to 18 inches of snowfall across higher elevations.||Thousands of people across northeast New Jersey lost power during this event as heavy snow accumulated on trees that still had partial to full foliage during mid-autumn. This caused extensive felling of trees and limbs across the region and damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage and power outages occurred due to 6 to 12 inches of heavy wet snow. The heavier amounts fell in the higher elevations.||In Cedar Grove, all lanes were closed due to downed wires on NJ 23 at County Road 617 around 730 pm EST. In North Caldwell and West Orange, multiple trees and power lines were reported down across the town by the evening of the 29th. In Millburn, all lanes were closed on NJ 124 west at Lakeview Avenue due to a downed tree in the early morning of the 30th. In Roseland, a child was injured when a tree branch fell on a child on Evelyn Road around noon on the 30th.

The low tide reached 1.84 feet below mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 1.90 feet below mean lower low water at Avalon (Cape May County), 1.80 feet below mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 2.10 feet below mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware, 1.91 feet below mean lower low water in Philadelphia, 1.89 feet below mean lower low water in Talcony (Burlington County) and 1.85 feet below mean lower low water in Burlington  (Burlington County). Blowout tides start at levels less than 1.80 feet below mean lower low water.
