EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with repeated torrential downpours caused flash flooding of roadways and streams in the western part of Cumberland County and affected Upper Deerfield Township and Bridgeton the hardest where two dams failed. This led to the evacuation of residents along the Cohansey River in Bridgeton, A county state of emergency was also declared. Over a dozen roadways were flooded and evacuations and water rescues occurred. Major roadways that were flooded and closed included State Routes 49 and 77. Many farm fields were submerged. Other hard hit municipalities included Greenwich, Stow Creek, Hopewell and Shiloh. Crops were also damaged, especially canning tomatoes. Damage was estimated at 20 million dollars. ||In Upper Deerfield Township, the torrential downpours started a domino effect of dam failures along the Cohansey River.  Seeley Lake was breached along Finley Road. The road was destroyed as the lake completely emptied. Downstream, the surge of water caused Sunset Lake to breach at West Park Drive in Bridgeton. This lead to the evacuation of approximately 150 residents along the Cohansey River within the city. A shelter was opened on Buckston Road. An approximately 1250 foot breach occurred along the river that flooded the Raceway within the Bridgeton City Park. The Cohanzick Zoo along the river was also flooded, but no animals were injured and the zoo itself was not seriously damaged. Flooding along Sunset Lake also led to the washout of a 75 foot by 100 foot section of Beebe Run Road within the city.  Event precipitation totals included 10.82 inches in Upper Deerfield and 10.63 inches in Seabrook.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the flash flooding rains, thunderstorms affected New Jersey during the daylight hours on the 14th. Some locally severe thunderstorms moved through southwest New Jersey during the first half of the afternoon on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in Upper Deerfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Irene moved north along the Atlantic coast and interacted with land, it weakened and made its second landfall as a Tropical Storm near Little Egg Inlet along the southeast NJ Coast on August 28, 2011 around 5:35 am EDT.  Tropical Storm Irene made its third landfall in New York City around 9:00 am.||Three deaths occurred:||A 40 year old male drowned while walking after his car became stranded in flood waters in Kearny (Hudson County).||A 39 year old male in Wanaque, Passaic County, NJ drowned while walking in the |woods when a sinkhole opened up and he was swept away into a nearby stream.||A 60 year old male in North Bergen, Hudson County, NJ died from a fall while cleaning out the gutters of his house.||Irene brought tropical storm force winds, destructive storm surge, and record breaking freshwater inland flooding across northeast NJ that resulted in 3 deaths, thousands of mandatory and voluntary evacuations along the coast and rivers from surge and freshwater flooding, and widespread power outages that lasted for up to 2 weeks in areas that were subsequently impacted by the remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Lee in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Irene moved north of the area, west winds were sustained at 39 knots with a peak gust to 53 knots measured by the ASOS at Newark Liberty Airport.||The peak wind gust measured by ASOS at Teterboro Airport was west at 42 knots.

While the worst tidal flooding occurred along tidal sections of the Delaware River, widespread minor tidal flooding also occurred in upper Delaware Bay. The high tide reached 7.56 feet above mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware (Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water). ||Tropical Storm Lee made landfall in Louisiana during the morning of the 4th and moved slowly northeast. An approaching cold front from the Mississippi Valley reached Lee by the morning of the 5th and transformed it to an extratropical system on the 5th in Mississippi. The same cold front acted as a conduit for Lee's moisture farther to the northeast. Showers and thunderstorms from that approaching cold front arrived during the evening and overnight on the 5th  and set off the first round of flash flooding rains in Northwest New Jersey.  The front then stalled just south of Delmarva on the 6th. A relative lull in the rain then occurred during the morning of the 6th. As the front started to back north into New Jersey as a warm front later in the day on the 6th, more bands of heavier rain returned that afternoon and particularly that evening. A blocking weather pattern then moved the frontal boundary little from the morning of the 7th into the morning of the 8th over New Jersey. Most of the precipitation on the 7th remained west of the state. Heavier rain formed north of the stalled frontal boundary in central and northern New Jersey during the evening of the 7th. A band of showers and thunderstorms with heavier rain then moved across the state during the first half of the day on the 8th. The frontal boundary started to drift offshore on the 8th and little if any Lee related precipitation fell in the state after that morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding forced the closure of sections of New Jersey State Route 70 and U.S. Route 130 in Camden County. The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 849 a.m. EDT through 431 p.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 3.71 feet at 1000 a.m. EDT. Entire event rainfall totals included 3.87 inches in Stratford, 2.79 inches in Winslow Township, 2.48 inches in Merchantville and 2.37 inches in Berlin Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rip current rescues occurred on Long Beach Island in Ocean County on the 12th, one near Barnegat Light and three in Long Beach Township. In all thirteen people were rescued. No serious injuries were reported. An onshore flow around a Canadian Maritimes high pressure system occurred on the 11th.

A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage and small creek flooding in central and eastern Monmouth Counties in Long Branch, Neptune, Ocean and Middletown Townships. Several major roads were flooded and closed and water rescues were performed. In Long Branch, five vehicles became stuck in flood waters.  In Neptune Township, both New Jersey State Routes 33 and 35 flooded. In Ocean Township, three roadways flooded and in Middletown Township, southbound New Jersey State Route 35 was closed as was Palmer Avenue between New Jersey State Routes 35 and 36.  Event precipitation totals included 5.55 inches in Ocean Township,  5.51 inches in Red Bank, 4.78 inches in Rumson, 4.66 inches in Long Branch, 4.29 inches in Middletown Township and 3.82 inches in Belmar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In addition to the flash flooding rains, thunderstorms affected New Jersey during the daylight hours on the 14th. Some locally severe thunderstorms moved through southwest New Jersey during the first half of the afternoon on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in the city of Bridgeton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As Hurricane Irene moved north along the Atlantic coast and interacted with land, it weakened and made its second landfall as a Tropical Storm near Little Egg Inlet along the southeast NJ Coast on August 28, 2011 around 5:35 am EDT.  Tropical Storm Irene made its third landfall in New York City around 9:00 am.||Three deaths occurred:||A 40 year old male drowned while walking after his car became stranded in flood waters in Kearny (Hudson County).||A 39 year old male in Wanaque, Passaic County, NJ drowned while walking in the |woods when a sinkhole opened up and he was swept away into a nearby stream.||A 60 year old male in North Bergen, Hudson County, NJ died from a fall while cleaning out the gutters of his house.||Irene brought tropical storm force winds, destructive storm surge, and record breaking freshwater inland flooding across northeast NJ that resulted in 3 deaths, thousands of mandatory and voluntary evacuations along the coast and rivers from surge and freshwater flooding, and widespread power outages that lasted for up to 2 weeks in areas that were subsequently impacted by the remnant moisture from Tropical Storm Lee in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As Irene moved north of the area, west winds were sustained at 39 knots with a peak gust to 53 knots measured by the ASOS at Newark Liberty Airport.||The peak wind gust measured by the ASOS at Essex County Airport in Caldwell was west at 39 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Lee that interacted with a stalled frontal boundary produced several days with periods of heavy rain across New Jersey from the 5th into the 8th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 8 inches, with lesser amounts in Atlantic, Cape May and eastern Ocean Counties. The heavy rain caused renewed flooding mainly to the west and northwest of the New Jersey Turnpike corridor with mainly moderate flooding along the main stem of the Delaware River with moderate to major flooding in the Passaic and Raritan Basins. Crests were higher on the Delaware River than with Irene as many sites had their highest crests since late June of 2006. The crests along the Delaware River were 2 to 4 feet higher than what occurred during Irene. Elsewhere, nearly all crests were lower than what occurred during Irene. Most of the reported damage occurred in counties bordering the Delaware River as 2 homes were destroyed, 24 suffered major damage, 249 suffered minor damage and 28 others were affected. There were approximately 1,000 people evacuated; other than Morris County, most of the other evacuations occurred in counties bordering the Delaware River. Throughout the state 162 roads were closed due to flooding. The freshwater surge also helped cause moderate tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River. Preliminary statewide damage was estimated at around 11.5 million dollars. The heavy rain and flooding also took its toll on crops. Farmers reported up to seventy-five percent losses of their pumpkin and tomato crops.||The combination of fresh water runoff and a weak southerly flow produced moderate tidal flooding during the evening high tide cycles from the 7th through the 9th and minor tidal flooding with all of the other high tide cycles from the evening of the 6th through overnight on the 11th. The highest tides occurred during the evening high tide cycle on the 8th.  In Burlington County, tidal flooding was reported in Delran and Cinnaminson.  In Camden County, the Admiral Wilson Boulevard flooded as did South Park Drive along the Cooper River in Collingswood. In Gloucester County, New Jersey State Route 44 was closed near the Repaupo Creek.||The evening high tide on the 8th in Philadelphia reached 9.41 feet above mean lower low water. The high tide reached 7.56 feet above mean lower low water at Reedy Island Delaware (Minor tidal flooding starts at 7.2 feet above mean lower low water). ||Tropical Storm Lee made landfall in Louisiana during the morning of the 4th and moved slowly northeast. An approaching cold front from the Mississippi Valley reached Lee by the morning of the 5th and transformed it to an extratropical system on the 5th in Mississippi. The same cold front acted as a conduit for Lee's moisture farther to the northeast. Showers and thunderstorms from that approaching cold front arrived during the evening and overnight on the 5th  and set off the first round of flash flooding rains in Northwest New Jersey.  The front then stalled just south of Delmarva on the 6th. A relative lull in the rain then occurred during the morning of the 6th. As the front started to back north into New Jersey as a warm front later in the day on the 6th, more bands of heavier rain returned that afternoon and particularly that evening. A blocking weather pattern then moved the frontal boundary little from the morning of the 7th into the morning of the 8th over New Jersey. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused widespread flooding in Sussex County. Many roadways were closed. Schools were closed on the 8th in Vernon and Sparta Townships. A few homes were flooded in Andover Township. Sections of New Jersey State Route 23 and 94 were closed in Franklin Township and Stockholm (Hardyston Township). The Flat Brook flooded a few homes in southwest Sussex County.||The Flat Brook at Flatbrookville had moderate flooding and was above its 6 foot flood stage from 903 a.m. EDT on the 7th through 905 a.m. EDT on the 9th.  It crested at 8.90 feet at 600 p.m. EDT on the 8th.  Entire event rainfall totals included 7.93 inches in Montague Township, 7.79 inches in Wantage Township, 7.79 inches in Sussex, 7.32 inches in Andover, 7.23 inches in Pellettown, 6.89 inches in High Point and 5.53 inches in Flatbrookville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing strong cold front and associated upper level trough triggered isolated severe thunderstorms across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 5-6 inch diameter tree limb was reported down at the intersection of Godwin Avenue and Joshua Lane in Wyckoff and a 4-5 inch diameter tree limb was reported down at the intersection of Belle Court and Birch Street in Midland Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and upper level short wave triggered severe thunderstorms across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down throughout Morsemere.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front and upper level short wave triggered severe thunderstorms across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were reported down throughout Leonia.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours led to flash flooding along the Culver Creek in Branchville. A State of Emergency was declared after the creek tore away the Mill Street Bridge in the borough. Ten homes had over eight feet of water in them, the most severely damaged were at the end of Mill Street. Residents from about 50 homes evacuated, with residents from all but one home being able to return at Noon EDT that day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal passage helped trigger thunderstorms preceding it across southeast New Jersey during the afternoon of the 15th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 40-year-old male construction worker was killed and two others were injured after they were struck by lightning while working on the Revel Casino Project in Atlantic City off of Connecticut Avenue. They were pouring concrete on the 7th floor when the bucket lift they were using was struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep cutoff low pressure system that was nearly stationary over the Ohio Valley for several days moved closer to the local area as its surface cold front approached from the west.||Moderate to heavy showers produced flooding on the Rahway River in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rahway, the North Branch of the Rahway River exceeded bankfull, which resulted in the closure of the Linden Ave. and St. Georges Ave. intersection due to flooding.  ||The South Branch of the Rahway River also exceeded bankfull, which resulted in the closure of the Inman Ave. and Jefferson Ave. intersection due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding across northwest New Jersey during the morning of the 28th. Event precipitation totals in the heavier band that extended from northern Hunterdon County northeastward through the border of Sussex and Morris Counties averaged 3 to 5 inches. The runoff from the heavy rain then caused some stream and smaller river flooding in northwest New Jersey that lasted into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding along many small streams in north central Hunterdon County. Stream flooding was reported in Lebanon, Glen Gardner, Clinton and Tewksbury.  There were a couple of camper rescues and vehicles were flooded. The runoff from the heavy rain also caused flooding along the South Branch of the Raritan River. Event precipitation totals included 3.99 inches in High Bridge, 3.94 inches in Clinton, 3.93 inches in Lebanon, 3.88 inches in Califon, 3.23 inches in Holland and 3.13 inches in Franklin.

The runoff from the heavy rain then caused some stream and smaller river flooding in northwest New Jersey that lasted into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding along smaller streams in southwestern Morris County in Washington and Mount Olive Townships. About a half dozen people had to be rescued from vehicles along River Road (South Branch of the Raritan River) in Mount Olive Township. Event precipitation totals included 4.99 inches in Mount Olive Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain caused flooding in northern Gloucester County including the Mantua Creek basin.  A 55-year-old woman drowned after she drove into the swollen creek. Her body was recovered on the 15th. In West Deptford, a 30 foot by 15 foot sink hole caused a basement masonry wall collapse on Ellwood Avenue. Near the Mantua Creek, the southern end of the Willow Woods Mobile Home Park on Crown Point Road (New Jersey State Route 44) was flooded and evacuated. The roadway remained closed into the 15th. Paradise Road in West Deptford was also closed. Event precipitation totals in the Mantua Creek drainage basin averaged 4 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot weather occurred on June 8th and 9th across New Jersey. High temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s both days, with some low 100s across southeastern New Jersey on the 9th. The 9th was the more humid of the two days with afternoon heat indices of 100F to around 105F. The 9th was also the hottest day for most places. There were a couple of occurrences of heat related illnesses reported in the central part of the state. Many schools and school districts closed early both days. Many districts also canceled after school classes and activities. Many communities opened cooling centers in libraries, senior centers and town halls. A cold front that triggered strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 9th moved through New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 10th and lowered both temperature and humidity levels.  ||Highest temperatures included 102 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 99 degrees in Wrightstown (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 98 degrees in Sicklerville (Camden County), Fort Monmouth (Monmouth County), Chatham (Morris County), Hillsborough (Somerset County) South Harrison (Gloucester County) and Trenton (Mercer County), 97 degrees in Woodbine (Cape May County), Cherry Hill (Cape May County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 96 degrees in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 95 degrees at West Cape May (Cape May County) and the Marina in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and 94 degrees in Walpack (Sussex County) and Stewartsville (Warren County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike took down two wires on the property of the Crystal Springs Aquatic Center in East Brunswick Township. One downed wire energized a fence. The facility closed for the rest of the day. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires near the Interstate 295 corridor in Hamilton Township. A Skywarn Spotter estimated wind gusts of 60 mph in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably hot weather occurred on June 8th and 9th across New Jersey. High temperatures reached the mid to upper 90s both days, with some low 100s across southeastern New Jersey on the 9th. The 9th was the more humid of the two days with afternoon heat indices of 100F to around 105F. The 9th was also the hottest day for most places. There were a couple of occurrences of heat related illnesses reported in the central part of the state. Many schools and school districts closed early both days. Many districts also canceled after school classes and activities. Many communities opened cooling centers in libraries, senior centers and town halls. A cold front that triggered strong to severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon and evening of the 9th moved through New Jersey during the first half of the day on the 10th and lowered both temperature and humidity levels.  ||Highest temperatures included 102 degrees in Toms River (Ocean County) and Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 99 degrees in Wrightstown (Burlington County) and Millville (Cumberland County), 98 degrees in Sicklerville (Camden County), Fort Monmouth (Monmouth County), Chatham (Morris County), Hillsborough (Somerset County) South Harrison (Gloucester County) and Trenton (Mercer County), 97 degrees in Woodbine (Cape May County), Cherry Hill (Cape May County) and New Brunswick (Middlesex County), 96 degrees in Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 95 degrees at West Cape May (Cape May County) and the Marina in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and 94 degrees in Walpack (Sussex County) and Stewartsville (Warren County).

A 15-year-old boy drowned after getting caught in a rip current near a jetty in Bradley Beach in Monmouth County during the late afternoon on the 9th. Two other people were also caught in the same rip current. A 28-year-old woman managed to pull herself onto the jetty while a 27-year-old man was rescued by two other men. The boy's body was recovered from the Shark River Inlet near the Ocean Avenue Bridge that separates Avon-By-The-Sea and Bradley Beach on the 14th.
Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a lee side trough and affected central New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and wires in Eatontown.  This severe thunderstorm was responsible for dozens of calls of downed trees in the southern half of the county.

There was one storm related death in Ocean County. A funnel cloud was sighted in Ocean County, but did not touch down. Jersey Central Power and Light reported about 10,000 homes and businesses lost power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and trees along the U.S. Route 206 corridor in Lawrence Township. Sections of the roadway were blocked and closed by the fallen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of low pressure riding along a nearly stationary frontal boundary across the local area caused several rounds of thunderstorms, which resulted in flash flooding throughout Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The intersection of Patterson St. and Washington Rd. was covered with four ft. of water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an unseasonably hot and humid air mass and an approaching cold front helped trigger a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that moved through New Jersey during the evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees, tree limbs and wires in Princeton Township. Cherry Valley Road was blocked between The Great Road and Province Line Road. Trees and wires were also knocked down on U.S. Route 206, Mercer Street, Crestview Road, Dempsey Avenue, Riverside Drive, Stuart Road, Terhune Road, Quaker Road and Cherry Hill Road. U.S. Route 206, Mercer Street, Cherry Valley and Stuart Roads were still closed the next morning.  In Princeton Borough, about a dozen trees were knocked down. One vehicle in the Borough Hall parking lot was damaged by a fallen tree. About 500 homes and businesses lost power in Mercer County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a wind gust of 62 mph during a severe thunderstorm in Florence Township. A few trees were also knocked down within the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall, which caused isolated flash flooding in Bergen County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms formed near a warm frontal boundary during the afternoon of the 11th across southern New Jersey, one of which became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped hail as large as quarters in Clayton.

The combination of a lee side trough at the surface, a weakening cold front approaching Pennsylvania and New Jersey and a northwest flow aloft helped produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening of the 19th in southern New Jersey. Hardest hit by the severe thunderstorms were Cumberland and Gloucester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning stroke a home in Elk Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and tree limbs in the Port Norris area of Commerical Township. One downed tree fell onto a home.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Hopewell Township, one onto State Route 49 (Shiloh Pike).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in the Newport area of Downe Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of another upper air disturbance and a weak surface trough triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped penny to quarter size hail in Morristown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across central and northern New Jersey during the early evening of the 29th. Hardest hit were Sussex, Burlington and Ocean Counties. About 37,000 Jersey Central Power and Light and Public Service Electric and Gas customers lost power in the state. Full power restoration did not occur until Noon EDT on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Howell Township in the Freewood Acres area between Interstate 195 and West Farms Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 54-year-old male was struck and killed by lightning while ducking under a tree during a thunderstorm to light a cigar in Hammonton. In 1963, the man's father was also struck and killed by lightning. A 38 year-old male cousin was seriously injured by the same lightning strike. The man felt a blast and remembered being thrown back a few feet before losing consciousness. He regained consciousness in the ambulance and was expected to recover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees in Sparta Township on and around West Mountain Road, Grandview Terrace and Sterling Hill Road. One property lost between fifteen and twenty trees and also suffered roof damage to its home. About 150 homes lost power. Power was fully restored by 3 p.m. EDT that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trough at the surface, a weakening cold front approaching Pennsylvania and New Jersey and a northwest flow aloft helped produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening of the 19th in southern New Jersey. Hardest hit by the severe thunderstorms were Cumberland and Gloucester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees, one onto a home in Lawrence Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail also fell from the severe thunderstorm in Sparta Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and started a barn fire on Lamington Road in Bedminster Township. Seven fire companies responded to the fire and it took about forty minutes to extinguish. Much of the hay in the barn was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a communications failure that affected the police, fire and rescue services in East Brunswick Township. The municipal building also suffered some structural damage by the lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike knocked out a transformer on the roof of the JFK Medical Center. It also caused a small smoky fire in a pipe in the wall of the hospital. Sixteen patients were moved off the pediatrics specialty care unit until the blaze was extinguished.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire destroyed a shed and its contents in Alexandria Township. Lightning struck a branch of a tree and then traveled to the roof of the shed where it ignited the fire. It took two fire companies about twenty minutes to extinguish the blaze. A snow blower, go cart and tools within the shed were all destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike to Lucy the Elephant in Margate caused significant damage to the tourist attraction's electrical and climate control system. The computer printers, air conditioning controllers, lighting and fire alarm controllers all suffered electrical damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trough at the surface, a weakening cold front approaching Pennsylvania and New Jersey and a northwest flow aloft helped produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening of the 19th in southern New Jersey. Hardest hit by the severe thunderstorms were Cumberland and Gloucester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees along Cornwell Drive in Upper Deerfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trough at the surface, a weakening cold front approaching Pennsylvania and New Jersey and a northwest flow aloft helped produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening of the 19th in southern New Jersey. Hardest hit by the severe thunderstorms were Cumberland and Gloucester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm uprooted trees, including weeping willows, knocked down some fences and sheds in Fortescue (Downe Township). The downed trees and wires caused scattered power outages throughout Cumberland County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Mansfield Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a lee side trough and affected central New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 6th. Multiple thunderstorms affected northwestern Burlington County where flash flooding occurred. Adjusted Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in parts of Florence, Mansfield and Bordentown Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding along the U.S. Route 206 corridor in Bordentown Township. A couple of water rescues occurred and no injuries were reported.  Adjusted Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in parts of Bordentown Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A runway anemometer at McGuire Air Force Base had a measured wind gust of 60 mph during a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous tree limbs and trees in Whiting (Manchester Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous tree limbs and trees in Browns Mills (Pemberton Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down about a half dozen poles and a couple of trees in Mount Laurel and caused power outages that lasted into the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The unseasonably very hot and humid air mass helped trigger isolated strong to severe thunderstorms over central New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. Public Service Electirc and Gas reported about 15,000 of its customers lost power in Burlington and Mercer Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail fell in Browns Mills (Pemberton Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most oppressive heat waves since mid July 1995 enveloped New Jersey from July 21st through the 24th. The heat was responsible for two deaths and hundreds of heat related injuries. Many locations had high temperatures that reached into the 100s. The most oppressive day was July 22nd when the combination of temperature and dew points pushed many afternoon heat index values to 110F to around 120F. While high temperatures on July 23rd were similar in the central and southern parts of the state, dew point temperatures and the heat index values were lower.||There were heat related deaths in Burlington and Mercer Counties. Both people were found indoors without air-conditioning. There were hundreds of cases heat exhaustion and other heat related illnesses. Health authorities were seeing younger people than typical. The largest concentration of heat related injuries occurred at the Vans Warped Tour stop at Monmouth Park in Oceanport (Monmouth County) on the 24th. Three hundred and one people were treated for heat exhaustion, twenty-seven were taken to hospitals, three were admitted. One child suffered heat exhaustion while participating in a rugby tournament in Morris County on the 23rd. | |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. Nearly every county in the state opened cooling centers. New Jersey activated its 211 information line. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended.  People flocked to the malls, movie theaters and pools to keep cool. Construction workers adjusted their work days and started early. Paving work was postponed.  Water and electrical service shutoffs were postponed. Home air-conditioning repairs, ice suppliers, automobile air conditioning repairs, chilly treat vendors all saw increases in demand as was an increase in water deliveries. Ironically the excessive heat caused a drop in people going to the beaches as even there it was too hot and humid.  ||Utilities urged people to conserve electricity and water while many were setting usage records. Overall there were not many heat related power. The PJM Interconnect (manages high voltage transmission systems) which includes Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland and New Jersey set an all time record usage of 158,000 megawatts at 5 p.m. EDT on July 21st.  Atlantic City Electric set a new usage record of 3,074 megawatts at 3 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. Public Service Electric and Gas had its second highest electrical usage on record also on the 22nd only behind August 1, 2006.|  |The lack of rain along with the heat was stressing both farm crops (corn and soybeans in particular) and livestock||Highest temperatures (all of which occurred on either the 22nd or 23rd in the southeast part of the state) included 106 degrees at the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, 105 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport and New Brusnwick (Middlesex County), 104 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County) and Chatham (Morris County), 103 degrees in Lakehurst (Ocean County), 102 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Sicklerville (Camden County), 101 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County, Pequest (Warren County) and Andover (Sussex County) and 100 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Wildwood (Cape May County).    The highest hourly heat index at the Atlantic City International Airport was 122 degrees on the 22nd. ||At the Atlantic City International Airport, the 105 degree high temperature was the second hottest day on record and hottest since June 28, 1969 (106 degrees)  and the 4th highest maximum temperature on record. The minimum temperature of 84 degrees on the 23rd was the warmest or highest  minimum temperature on record.  The monthly average temperature of 81.0 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 20 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high is second only to 1983 with 21 days. ||At the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 80.9 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 21 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high also established a new record. | |This heat wave helped make July 2011 the third hottest July on record for the state of New Jersey with a statewide average temperature of 78.4 degrees. | |A weak cold front ended the heat wave on the 25th.

Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a lee side trough and affected central New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 6th. Multiple thunderstorms affected northwestern Burlington County where flash flooding occurred. Adjusted Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in parts of Florence, Mansfield and Bordentown Townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused small stream and roadway flash flooding in Florence Township. Adjusted Doppler Radar storm total estimates were 3 to 4 inches in parts of Florence Township. A Cocorahs observer in neighboring Chesterfield Township measured 2.49 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck four homes in Toms River Township, but caused little structural damage. The lighting caused about 2,000 JCP&L customers to lose power in ten municipalities within Ocean County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and started a roof fire in an unoccupied home on Fathom Avenue in the Ocean Acres section of Stafford Township. Four fire companies responded and confined the fire to the west side of the home's roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail fell in Manalapan Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to the size of quarters fell from a severe thunderstorm in Jackson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front acted as a focus for strong to severe thunderstorms during the early morning of the 3rd in northwestern New Jersey and during the late afternoon and early evening of the 3rd across central New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the large hail, the severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires within Jackson Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous large tree limbs and some wires in Manchester Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a lee side trough and affected central New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees in Wall Township and caused road closures. Ocean Road was closed near Bailey Corner Road. Allaire Road was closed near South Manor Court. A transformer fire closed Eighteenth Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most oppressive heat waves since mid July 1995 enveloped New Jersey from July 21st through the 24th. The heat was responsible for two deaths and hundreds of heat related injuries. Many locations had high temperatures that reached into the 100s. The most oppressive day was July 22nd when the combination of temperature and dew points pushed many afternoon heat index values to 110F to around 120F. While high temperatures on July 23rd were similar in the central and southern parts of the state, dew point temperatures and the heat index values were lower.||There were heat related deaths in Burlington and Mercer Counties. Both people were found indoors without air-conditioning. There were hundreds of cases heat exhaustion and other heat related illnesses. Health authorities were seeing younger people than typical. The largest concentration of heat related injuries occurred at the Vans Warped Tour stop at Monmouth Park in Oceanport (Monmouth County) on the 24th. Three hundred and one people were treated for heat exhaustion, twenty-seven were taken to hospitals, three were admitted. One child suffered heat exhaustion while participating in a rugby tournament in Morris County on the 23rd. | |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. Nearly every county in the state opened cooling centers. New Jersey activated its 211 information line. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended.  People flocked to the malls, movie theaters and pools to keep cool. Construction workers adjusted their work days and started early. Paving work was postponed.  Water and electrical service shutoffs were postponed. Home air-conditioning repairs, ice suppliers, automobile air conditioning repairs, chilly treat vendors all saw increases in demand as was an increase in water deliveries. Ironically the excessive heat caused a drop in people going to the beaches as even there it was too hot and humid.  ||Utilities urged people to conserve electricity and water while many were setting usage records. Overall there were not many heat related power. The PJM Interconnect (manages high voltage transmission systems) which includes Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland and New Jersey set an all time record usage of 158,000 megawatts at 5 p.m. EDT on July 21st.  Atlantic City Electric set a new usage record of 3,074 megawatts at 3 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. Public Service Electric and Gas had its second highest electrical usage on record also on the 22nd only behind August 1, 2006.|  |The lack of rain along with the heat was stressing both farm crops (corn and soybeans in particular) and livestock||Highest temperatures (all of which occurred on either the 22nd or 23rd in the southeast part of the state) included 106 degrees at the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, 105 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport and New Brusnwick (Middlesex County), 104 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County) and Chatham (Morris County), 103 degrees in Lakehurst (Ocean County), 102 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Sicklerville (Camden County), 101 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County, Pequest (Warren County) and Andover (Sussex County) and 100 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Wildwood (Cape May County).    The highest hourly heat index at the Atlantic City International Airport was 122 degrees on the 22nd. ||At the Atlantic City International Airport, the 105 degree high temperature was the second hottest day on record and hottest since June 28, 1969 (106 degrees)  and the 4th highest maximum temperature on record. The minimum temperature of 84 degrees on the 23rd was the warmest or highest  minimum temperature on record.  The monthly average temperature of 81.0 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 20 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high is second only to 1983 with 21 days. ||At the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 80.9 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 21 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high also established a new record. | |This heat wave helped make July 2011 the third hottest July on record for the state of New Jersey with a statewide average temperature of 78.4 degrees. | |A weak cold front ended the heat wave on the 25th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in Montclair, causing a fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a lee side trough and affected central New Jersey during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and several tree limbs in Springfield Township. Mount Pleasant Road was closed as was County Route 537 between State Route 68 and Wrightstown-Georgetown Road. There were also downed tree limbs along State Route 68 within the township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a large tree on Bromley Boulevard in Burlington County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Galloway Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and large tree limbs in Medford and Southampton Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance spawned strong, but sub-severe, storms that brought down isolated trees. Measured winds at observing stations only had gusts in the 45 to 50 mph range as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto a house along Darlington Road, and a tree fell across Route 202 at Ramapo College in Mahwah. The trees were brought down due to a combination of heavy rain and strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching upper level disturbance and a stationary front in the vicinity produced a couple of severe storms with damaging winds in Union and Bergen Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were reported to be blocking the intersection of La Grande Avenue and First Street in Fanwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an approaching upper level disturbance and a stationary front in the vicinity produced a couple of severe storms with damaging winds in Union and Bergen Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Ripplewood Road in Upper Saddle River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance spawned strong, but sub-severe, storms that brought down isolated trees. Measured winds at observing stations only had gusts in the 45 to 50 mph range as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down along Carletondale Road and another along Margaret King Avenue in Ringwood. The trees were brought down due to a combination of heavy rain and strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing pre-frontal trough and cold front, coupled with a mid level disturbance, produced an isolated severe thunderstorm in Passaic County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread trees were reported down in the Pinecliff Lake Community in West Milford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Clarksburg in Millstone Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in Jackson Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down trees and wires in the East Freehold section of Freehold Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance spawned strong, but sub-severe, storms that brought down isolated trees. Measured winds at observing stations only had gusts in the 45 to 50 mph range as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A carnival tent pole was blown down along Lenox Avenue, and a tree limb fell onto power lines along Hilltop Court in Pompton Lakes. The tree and pole were brought down due to a combination of heavy rain and strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance spawned strong, but sub-severe, storms that brought down isolated trees. Measured winds at observing stations only had gusts in the 45 to 50 mph range as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were reported down due to a combination of heavy rainfall and strong winds along Woodland Avenue in Verona, including one into a fence.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing upper level disturbance spawned strong, but sub-severe, storms that brought down isolated trees. Measured winds at observing stations only had gusts in the 45 to 50 mph range as the storms moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell down at the intersection of Myrtle Road and Grove Street and on Darlington Avenue near the Mahwah Border in Ramsey. The trees were brought down due to a combination of heavy rain and strong gusty winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped penny to quarter size hail in Whitehouse Station (Readington Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a house fire in Ocean View (Dennis Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck and injured a man standing on a porch during a thunderstorm on Townhouse Lane in Little Egg Harbor Township. He complained about chest and leg pain as well as overall weakness. Another lightning strike temporarily knocked off communications at the township police department after a roof antenna was struck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the first half of the evening of the 7th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the third time in 2011, the water treatment plant in Allentown Borough was struck by lightning, This lightning strike fried computerized controls and caused about an estimated $40,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of low pressure riding north along a very slowly eastward moving cold front produced the dual threat of both severe weather and flash flooding across New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was videotaped over the Wharton State Forest. An aerial reconnaissance of the area did not show any tornadic type damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trough at the surface, a weakening cold front approaching Pennsylvania and New Jersey and a northwest flow aloft helped produce strong to locally severe thunderstorms during the late afternoon into the evening of the 19th in southern New Jersey. Hardest hit by the severe thunderstorms were Cumberland and Gloucester Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning stroke and the ensuing fire damaged a home in Folsom Borough. No injuries were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive heat between 100 and 105 degrees, along with heat indices in excess of 105 degrees occurred for a couple of days. The heat index was as high as 116 degrees at 3 PM and 5 PM at Newark International Airport (KEWR) on July 22nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Waves of low pressure riding north along a very slowly eastward moving cold front produced the dual threat of both severe weather and flash flooding across New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger numerous thunderstorms during the second half of the afternoon into the early evening of the 8th in Camden and Gloucester Counties. Similar conditions occurred across eastern Morris County. Storm totals reached between 3 and 4 inches and caused considerable highway and poor drainage flooding as well as flash flooding of smaller streams and then later flooding along the Cooper River. Because this coincided with a Friday afternoon, they heavy rain and flooding greatly compounded the commute home and toward shore points in the local Philadelphia Metropolitan Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage and small stream flooding in central Gloucester County in and around Glassboro. A Skywarn spotter measured an event total of 4.00 inches in Glaassboro.  Other Gloucester County precipitation event totals included 3.07 inches in Mantua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One of the most oppressive heat waves since mid July 1995 enveloped New Jersey from July 21st through the 24th. The heat was responsible for two deaths and hundreds of heat related injuries. Many locations had high temperatures that reached into the 100s. The most oppressive day was July 22nd when the combination of temperature and dew points pushed many afternoon heat index values to 110F to around 120F. While high temperatures on July 23rd were similar in the central and southern parts of the state, dew point temperatures and the heat index values were lower.||There were heat related deaths in Burlington and Mercer Counties. Both people were found indoors without air-conditioning. There were hundreds of cases heat exhaustion and other heat related illnesses. Health authorities were seeing younger people than typical. The largest concentration of heat related injuries occurred at the Vans Warped Tour stop at Monmouth Park in Oceanport (Monmouth County) on the 24th. Three hundred and one people were treated for heat exhaustion, twenty-seven were taken to hospitals, three were admitted. One child suffered heat exhaustion while participating in a rugby tournament in Morris County on the 23rd. | |To combat the heat, many counties, cities and municipalities opened cooling centers. Nearly every county in the state opened cooling centers. New Jersey activated its 211 information line. The hours of air-conditioned senior citizen centers were extended.  People flocked to the malls, movie theaters and pools to keep cool. Construction workers adjusted their work days and started early. Paving work was postponed.  Water and electrical service shutoffs were postponed. Home air-conditioning repairs, ice suppliers, automobile air conditioning repairs, chilly treat vendors all saw increases in demand as was an increase in water deliveries. Ironically the excessive heat caused a drop in people going to the beaches as even there it was too hot and humid.  ||Utilities urged people to conserve electricity and water while many were setting usage records. Overall there were not many heat related power. The PJM Interconnect (manages high voltage transmission systems) which includes Pennsylvania, Delaware, Maryland and New Jersey set an all time record usage of 158,000 megawatts at 5 p.m. EDT on July 21st.  Atlantic City Electric set a new usage record of 3,074 megawatts at 3 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. Public Service Electric and Gas had its second highest electrical usage on record also on the 22nd only behind August 1, 2006.|  |The lack of rain along with the heat was stressing both farm crops (corn and soybeans in particular) and livestock||Highest temperatures (all of which occurred on either the 22nd or 23rd in the southeast part of the state) included 106 degrees at the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, 105 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport and New Brusnwick (Middlesex County), 104 degrees in Somerville (Somerset County) and Chatham (Morris County), 103 degrees in Lakehurst (Ocean County), 102 degrees in Belmar (Monmouth County) and Sicklerville (Camden County), 101 degrees in Lumberton (Burlington County, Pequest (Warren County) and Andover (Sussex County) and 100 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County) and Wildwood (Cape May County).    The highest hourly heat index at the Atlantic City International Airport was 122 degrees on the 22nd. ||At the Atlantic City International Airport, the 105 degree high temperature was the second hottest day on record and hottest since June 28, 1969 (106 degrees)  and the 4th highest maximum temperature on record. The minimum temperature of 84 degrees on the 23rd was the warmest or highest  minimum temperature on record.  The monthly average temperature of 81.0 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 20 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high is second only to 1983 with 21 days. ||At the Mercer County Airport in Trenton, the monthly average temperature of 80.9 degrees was not only the warmest July on record, but also the warmest month ever. The 21 days in which the maximum temperature reached 90 degrees or high also established a new record. | |This heat wave helped make July 2011 the third hottest July on record for the state of New Jersey with a statewide average temperature of 78.4 degrees. | |A weak cold front ended the heat wave on the 25th.

A slowly southward sinking cold front that entered northern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey during the early afternoon of the 24th helped trigger strong thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening of the 24th across New Jersey with isolated severe thunderstorms in the southern half of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in Ocean City along Central and Ocean Avenues as well as along the beach. Part of the roof was torn away from the Seabreeze Apartments on Ocean Avenue. The roof of the Sun Aqua motel was also dislodged. The roof debris damaged a nearby condominium as well as the windshield of several vehicles. On Central Avenue, three homes suffered partial roof collapses and facade damage. The debris hit homes across the street. A utility pole collapsed. Wind damage also occurred to the 5th Street ballfield. On the beach itself, thirteen lifeguard stands were destroyed as they went tumbling down the beach. About a dozen beach patrol surf boats went cartwheeling down the beach also. Six were totally damaged. One was found in Strathmere. A wind gust of 66 mph was recorded within Ocean City. No serious injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across central and northern New Jersey during the early evening of the 29th. Hardest hit were Sussex, Burlington and Ocean Counties. About 37,000 Jersey Central Power and Light and Public Service Electric and Gas customers lost power in the state. Full power restoration did not occur until Noon EDT on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees, tree limbs and wires in Willingboro Township. There were about 50 thunderstorm related calls throughout the township. One downed tree on Twig Court fell through a home and damaged a vehicle. Westbound Garfield Road remained closed through the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger strong to severe thunderstorms across central and northern New Jersey during the early evening of the 29th. Hardest hit were Sussex, Burlington and Ocean Counties. About 37,000 Jersey Central Power and Light and Public Service Electric and Gas customers lost power in the state. Full power restoration did not occur until Noon EDT on the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful and damaging microburst occurred in Vernon Township during the early evening of the 29th. Estimated maximum wind speeds were 90 mph. Wind damage began near Silver Fox Lane and moved southeast for about two miles and ended near the intersection of State Route 94 and Sand Hill Road. Most of the wind damage was downed trees, estimated at over one thousand as well as downed wires. Several homes sustained roof damage from the combination of either trees falling on them, or the winds themselves. In the Tall Timbers Mobile Home Park, a number of trees also fell on vehicles and damaged mobile homes. One home on Edsall Drive suffered significant roof damage. The family was taken in by the Crystal Springs Resort. A few other families in the area had to leave their home because of wind damage.  An amateur radio antenna on Edsall Drive was bent in half.  In addition to Edsall Drive, tree wind damage was extensive on Black Walnut Mountain Road, Basswood Drive and Shebe Drive. About 2,000 Jersey Central Power and Light Customers lost power, 500 still did not have power restored on the afternoon of the 30th.

Flash flooding was also reported along Federal Street and the Admiral Wilson Boulevard. Flash flooding was also reported on the Admiral Wilson Boulevard in Pennsauken Township as well as State Route 38. In Gloucester City, U.S. Route 130 was closed at Interstate 76. In Bellmawr Borough, Southbound New Jersey State Route 42 (North-South Freeway) was closed due to flooding just south of Interstate 295. Eastbound 295 itself was closed due to flooding at Creek Road. ||In Pennsauken, a Skywarn spotter measure 1.75 inches of rain within 20 minutes. Event precipitation totals within Camden County included 3.82 inches in Berlin, 2.93 inches in Winslow and 1.97 inches in Merchantville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While all the highest crests with Irene occurred in August, river and creek flooding persisted into September in parts of Atlantic, Burlington, Cumberland, Morris and Salem Counties. Flooding in Morris County persisted the longest into the 5th and ended just before the flooding associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the second time within a month, the Great Egg Harbor River at Folsom had record breaking major flooding. It was above its 6 foot flood stage from 1005 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 415 a.m. EDT on September 1st. It crested at 8.27 feet at 1115 p.m. EDT on August 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the heavy rain caused the greatest flooding along the Raccoon Creek since August 10, 1967. The Raccoon Creek at Swedesboro had its second highest crest on record since that date. The creek was above its 13 foot flood stage from 127 p.m. EDT on the 14th  through 907 a.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 15.96 feet at 715 p.m. EDT on the 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of another upper air disturbance and a weak surface trough triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike ignited a fire at a house on York Street in Lambertville. Lightning struck and went through the wiring of the home, found a weak spot in a crawl space underneath the first floor and started the fire.  A water pipe also burst inside the house. No injuries were reported, but the two residents of the house were provided shelter for the evening. The residents next door were also evacuated  due to the heavy smoke.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of another upper air disturbance and a weak surface trough triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that affected New Jersey during the late afternoon and early evening of the 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down four trees and two wires onto roadways in Madison Township. It caused power outages in the downtown area of the township as well as causing about a dozen traffic lights to stop functioning. A wind gust of 50 mph was measured in neighboring Chatham Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While all the highest crests with Irene occurred in August, river and creek flooding persisted into September in parts of Atlantic, Burlington, Cumberland, Morris and Salem Counties. Flooding in Morris County persisted the longest into the 5th and ended just before the flooding associated with the remnants of Tropical Storm Lee began.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pompton River had major flooding and record breaking crests throughout its entire length, surpassing the flooding that occurred during April of 1984. The river at Pompton Plains receded below flood stage before the start of September.  The Pompton River at Ryerson Road crested at a record breaking 20.47 feet at 1140 p.m. EDT on August 28th. It was above its 12 foot flood stage from 713 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 602 a.m. EDT on September 3rd. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at Midwood crested at a record breaking 17.31 feet at 630 p.m. EDT on August 29th. It was above its 7.5 foot flood stage from 634 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 845 p.m. EDT on September 5th. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at the Mountainview Bridge crested at a record breaking 16.08 feet at 420 p.m. EDT on August 29th. It was above its 8 foot flood stage from 908 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 739 a.m. EDT on September 4th. The Passaic River at Millington and Pine Brook had major flooding and record breaking crests. The Passaic River at Chatham had moderate flooding. The Passaic River at Millington record breaking crest of 10.26 feet occurred at 1 p.m. EDT on August 29th. It was above its 8 foot flood stage from 708 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 407 a.m. EDT on September 1st. The Passaic River at Chatham was above its 6 foot flood stage from 232 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 930 a.m. EDT on September 3rd. It crested at 7.76 feet at 1245 p.m. EDT on August 29th. The Passaic River at Pine Brook record breaking crest of 24.12 feet occurred at 730 a.m. EDT on August 30th. It was above its 19 foot flood stage from 730 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 1115 p.m. EDT on September 5th, just before the rains from Tropical Storm Lee arrived. The Passaic River was the last river and Pine Brook was the last location on the river to go below flood stage. Just downstream of Morris County along the Passaic and Union County line, the Passaic River at Two Bridges also had major flooding and a record breaking crest of 17.17 feet at 515 a.m. EDT on August 30th. It was above its 9 foot flood stage from 7 a.m. EDT on August 28th through 815 a.m. EDT on September 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While all the highest crests with Irene occurred in August, river and creek flooding persisted into September in parts of Atlantic, Burlington, Cumberland, Morris and Salem Counties. EVENT_NARRATIVE: For the second time within a month, the Maurice River at Norma had major flooding and set the second highest crest on record. The river at the Norma gage was above its 4 foot flood stage from 1115 p.m. EDT on August 27th through 1030 a.m. EDT on September 2nd.  It crested at 7.72 feet at 400 p.m. EDT on August 29th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front helped trigger some strong to locally severe thunderstorms and also one confirmed tornado across central and southern New Jersey during the afternoon of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado touched down in Millstone Township in Monmouth County. The tornado initially touched down north of Buono Farm and tracked northeast where it crossed New Jersey State Route 33.and damaged a flag pole and a business fencing.  A barn was damaged on Prodelin Way. Numerous trees and some wires were knocked down along its path, especially on Prodelin and Arrowhead Ways and Bergen Mills Road. The tornado moved along Arrowhead Way before it lifted. The tornado's approximate path length was 1.7 miles, maximum path width of 50 yards and estimated maximum wind speed of 70 mph. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the torrential downpours, especially in the headwaters of the Great Egg Harbor River, led to record breaking and major flooding at the Folsom gage site on the 16th. The Great Egg Harbor River at Folsom was above its 6 foot flood stage from 231 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 415 p.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 8.18 feet at 530 a.m. EDT on the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with repeated torrential downpours caused flash flooding with damaging effects across eastern Salem County, especially in Pittsgrove Township. A county state of emergency was declared. At least one water rescue was reported. Flooding was reported in Alloway, Pittsgrove, Elmer and Upper Pittsgrove Townships. Fourteen roadways, nine bridges and many culverts were damaged, most in Pittsgrove Township. Some families were evacuated in Pittsgrove Township around Palatine and Centerton Lakes. Two bridges washed away in the township on Greenville and Burlington Roads. It was expected to take six to twelve months for both bridges to be replaced. Two other township roadways remained closed into the 19th. In spite of being drained prior to the rain, Centeron Lake filled and flooded. A few homes were half submerged by flood waters along Palatine Lake. In Elmer, Elmer Lake also flooded. Event precipitation totals included 9.64 inches in Elmer and 5.89 inches in Woodstown.

It crested at 13.35 feet at 245 a.m. EDT.

The lightning traveled down the side of the home and entered into it through the metal dryer vent and set the lint on fire and burnt a hose. It also tripped the home's circuit breakers. Smoke filled the basement, but the firefighters' quick response confined the fire to the dryer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused small stream and poor drainage flash flooding across northwest New Jersey during the morning of the 28th. Event precipitation totals in the heavier band that extended from northern Hunterdon County northeastward through the border of Sussex and Morris Counties averaged 3 to 5 inches. The runoff from the heavy rain then caused some stream and smaller river flooding in northwest New Jersey that lasted into the 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from the heavier rain that fell earlier in the day on the 28th caused minor flooding along the South Branch of the Raritan River in Hunterdon County.  The South Branch of the Raritan River at High Bridge was above its 10 foot flood stage from 734 a.m. EDT through 815 p.m. EDT on the 28th. It crested at 11.18 feet at 3 p.m. EDT.  Farther downstream, the South Branch of the Raritan River at Stanton was above its 8 foot flood stage from 632 a.m. EDT on the 28th through 200 a.m. EDT on the 29th. It crested at 9.69 feet at 945 a.m. EDT on the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 24-year-old man drowned in Seaside Heights (Ocean County) near the Casino Pier after he was caught in a rip current shortly after 5 p.m. EDT on the 14th.  He was swimming with a 21-year-old woman when they became caught in the rip current. They were both struck by a wave. While the woman was pushed back to shore, the man was dragged farther out to sea. His body was found off the north side of the pier around 537 p.m. EDT and was pronounced dead a short time later.

A series of thunderstorms preceding a cold front dropped three to seven inches of rain across a wide swath of New Jersey (less along most of the coast) from overnight on the 13th into the day on the 14th. But in southern Gloucester, Eastern Salem and Western Cumberland County, rainfall amounts reached seven to eleven inches. Scattered thunderstorms occurred into the 15th and morning of the 16th. This slowed the recession of rivers and streams in the state. The combined event caused severe flash flooding with dam breaks in southwestern New Jersey and flash flooding and flooding across central and northern New Jersey.  Damage estimates reached around 25 million dollars, most of which occurred in Salem and Cumberland Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of roadways and streams in northeastern Morris County during the morning of the 14th. The runoff flooding continued into the morning of the 16th. The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 1005 a.m. EDT on the 14th through 817 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 3.13 feet at 1050  p.m. EDT on the 14th. Event precipitation totals included 4.45 inches in Long Hill Township, 4.08 inches in Denville, 3.79 inches in Morris Township, 3.75 inches in Chatham and 3.47 inches in Parsippany.
