EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Five days of showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain culminated with flash flooding of smaller streams in northeastern Morris County during the late afternoon and early evening of the 18th and then larger stream and river flooding in eastern Morris County that lasted into the 22nd. Minor flooding occurred along the Rockaway and Passaic Rivers and moderate flooding occurred along the lower stretches of the Pompton River. Five day event precipitation totals averaged 3.5 to 6.0 inches. The heaviest rain fell during the afternoon and early evening on the 18th with precipitation totals of around two inches.||The periods of heavy rain were caused by a persistent and blocky weather pattern which forced a low pressure system over the southeastern United States to only slowly move to the northeast. The surface and its upper level low pressure system did not reach New York State until the morning of the 20th. The persistent southeast flow coupled with some of the highest astronomical tides of the year also produced around five days of widespread minor tidal flooding during the evening and overnight high tide cycles from the 15th through the 19th. The highest tides occurred during the evening and overnight high tide cycle on the 17th. The highest tides included 7.66 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 6.52 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 7.67 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County), 7.86 feet above mean lower low water at Reedy Island, Delaware and 8.73 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Five consecutive days of periods of heavy rain led to flooding of brooks and rivers in the eastern part of Morris County. The Passaic River at Pine Brook was above its 19 foot flood stage from 700 a.m. EDT on the 19th through 945 p.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 19.68 feet at 830 a.m. EDT on the 20th. Farther downstream, the Passaic River at Circle Burners or Lincoln Park crested at 6.42 feet at 1005 p.m. EDT on the 20th. Flood stage is 5.5 feet.  The lower areas of the Pompton River had the worst flooding with this event as flood stages reached moderate levels. Nevertheless, this was less flooding than what occurred in mid March and mid April of this year. The Pompton River at Ryerson Road was above its 12 foot flood stage from 900 a.m. EDT on the 19th through 952 a.m. EDT on the 21st. It crested at 13.73 feet at 300 a.m. EDT on the 20th. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at Midwood was above its 7.5 foot flood stage from 759 a.m. EDT on the 19th through 845 a.m. EDT on the 22nd. It crested at 9.44 feet at 530 a.m. EDT on the 20th. ||The East Ditch at Beaver Brook Road was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 753 p.m. EDT on the 18th through 1138 a.m. EDT on the 19th. It crested at 3.06 feet at 315 a.m. EDT on the 19th. The Rockaway River above the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1003 p.m. EDT on the 18th through 855 p.m. EDT on the 19th. It crested at 5.53 feet at 7 a.m. EDT on the 19th. The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 445 p.m. EDT on the 18th through 730 a.m. EDT on the 21st. It crested at 6.20 feet at 1145 a.m. EDT on the 19th.||Event precipitation totals included 5.85 inches in Denville, 5.81 inches in Boonton, 5.71 inches in Rockaway, 5.58 inches in Morris, 4.98 inches in Jefferson, 4.90 inches in Parsippany, 3.75 inches in Mine Hill, 3.72 inches in Mount Olive, 3.71 inches in Long Hill, 3.70 inches in Chatham and 3.54 inches in Randolph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in New Jersey and stream and river flooding mainly in west central and northern New Jersey. Major flooding occurred again in sections of the Passaic River Basin.  The rain began during the morning of the 16th and fell at its heaviest during thunderstorms on the evening of the 16th. The rain ended during the early morning of the 17th. Precipitation totals averaged 2.0  to 4.0 inches along the Interstate 95 corridor and Morris County and generally 1 to 2 inches elsewhere in New Jersey.||The heavy rain was caused by a deep low pressure system that emerged from the Rockies on the 14th and moved northeast and reached the Great Lakes on the morning of the 16th and then north into Canada that evening. A strong high pressure system preceded it across southeast Canada and both pumped considerable moisture into the region from the Gulf of Mexico and the Atlantic Ocean. The rain ended shortly after the low's occluded front moved through Pennsylvania.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the tidal flooding along the Delaware River, the heavy rain caused the Raccoon Creek to flood inland. The Raccoon Creek near Swedesboro was above its 13 foot flood stage from 341 a.m. EDT through 1052 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 13.34 feet at 6 a.m. EDT. Event precipitation totals included 1.94 inches in Franklin.
